Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy0 line6

** What does this project do? copy0 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy0 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy0 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy0 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy0 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy0 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy0 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy0 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy0 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy0 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy0 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy0 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy0 line26
   within Emacs. copy0 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy0 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy0 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy0 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy0 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy0 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy0 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy0 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy0 line36
to share logic between them. copy0 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy0 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy0 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy0 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy0 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy0 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy0 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy0 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy0 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy0 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy0 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy0 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy0 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy0 line51
unambiguous way. copy0 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy0 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy0 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy0 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy0 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy0 line58

** Architecture copy0 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy0 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy0 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy0 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy0 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy0 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy0 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy0 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy0 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy0 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy0 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy0 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy0 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy0 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy0 line78
=org=. copy0 line79

* Project State copy0 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy0 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy0 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy0 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy0 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy0 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy0 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy0 line91
      - [X] Links copy0 line92
      - [X] Text links copy0 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy0 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy0 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy0 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy0 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy0 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy0 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy0 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy0 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy0 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy0 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy0 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy0 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy0 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy0 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy0 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy0 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy0 line112

* Development copy0 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy0 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy0 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy0 line118
suite expectations. copy0 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy0 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy0 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy0 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy0 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy0 line125

** Prerequisites copy0 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy0 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy0 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy0 line132

** Testing copy0 line134

Running the tests: copy0 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy0 line138
# Clojure copy0 line139
lein test copy0 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy0 line141
npm install copy0 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy0 line143
lein doo node once copy0 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy0 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy0 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy0 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy0 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy0 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy0 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy0 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy0 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy0 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy0 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy0 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy0 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy0 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy0 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy0 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy0 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy0 line174
lein deps copy0 line175
npm install copy0 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy0 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy0 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy0 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy0 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy0 line183
lein test copy0 line184
lein doo node once copy0 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy0 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy0 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy0 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy0 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy0 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy0 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy0 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy0 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy0 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy0 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy0 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy0 line201
a Lisp before. copy0 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy0 line204

* Usage copy0 line225
  :PROPERTIES:

or Java. Other targets which are hosted on the JVM or on JavaScript copy0 line231

 #+BEGIN_SRC clojure :exports both copy0 line236

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy2 line6

** What does this project do? copy2 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy2 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy2 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy2 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy2 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy2 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy2 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy2 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy2 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy2 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy2 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy2 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy2 line26
   within Emacs. copy2 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy2 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy2 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy2 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy2 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy2 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy2 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy2 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy2 line36
to share logic between them. copy2 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy2 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy2 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy2 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy2 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy2 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy2 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy2 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy2 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy2 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy2 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy2 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy2 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy2 line51
unambiguous way. copy2 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy2 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy2 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy2 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy2 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy2 line58

** Architecture copy2 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy2 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy2 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy2 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy2 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy2 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy2 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy2 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy2 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy2 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy2 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy2 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy2 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy2 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy2 line78
=org=. copy2 line79

* Project State copy2 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy2 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy2 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy2 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy2 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy2 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy2 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy2 line91
      - [X] Links copy2 line92
      - [X] Text links copy2 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy2 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy2 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy2 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy2 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy2 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy2 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy2 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy2 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy2 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy2 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy2 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy2 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy2 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy2 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy2 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy2 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy2 line112

* Development copy2 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy2 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy2 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy2 line118
suite expectations. copy2 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy2 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy2 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy2 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy2 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy2 line125

** Prerequisites copy2 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy2 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy2 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy2 line132

** Testing copy2 line134

Running the tests: copy2 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy2 line138
# Clojure copy2 line139
lein test copy2 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy2 line141
npm install copy2 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy2 line143
lein doo node once copy2 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy2 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy2 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy2 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy2 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy2 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy2 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy2 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy2 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy2 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy2 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy2 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy2 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy2 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy2 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy2 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy2 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy2 line174
lein deps copy2 line175
npm install copy2 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy2 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy2 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy2 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy2 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy2 line183
lein test copy2 line184
lein doo node once copy2 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy2 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy2 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy2 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy2 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy2 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy2 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy2 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy2 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy2 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy2 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy2 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy2 line201
a Lisp before. copy2 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy2 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy2 line206

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy4 line6

** What does this project do? copy4 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy4 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy4 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy4 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy4 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy4 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy4 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy4 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy4 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy4 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy4 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy4 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy4 line26
   within Emacs. copy4 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy4 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy4 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy4 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy4 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy4 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy4 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy4 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy4 line36
to share logic between them. copy4 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy4 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy4 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy4 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy4 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy4 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy4 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy4 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy4 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy4 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy4 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy4 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy4 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy4 line51
unambiguous way. copy4 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy4 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy4 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy4 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy4 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy4 line58

** Architecture copy4 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy4 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy4 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy4 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy4 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy4 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy4 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy4 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy4 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy4 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy4 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy4 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy4 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy4 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy4 line78
=org=. copy4 line79

* Project State copy4 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy4 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy4 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy4 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy4 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy4 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy4 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy4 line91
      - [X] Links copy4 line92
      - [X] Text links copy4 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy4 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy4 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy4 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy4 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy4 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy4 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy4 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy4 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy4 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy4 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy4 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy4 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy4 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy4 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy4 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy4 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy4 line112

* Development copy4 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy4 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy4 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy4 line118
suite expectations. copy4 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy4 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy4 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy4 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy4 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy4 line125

** Prerequisites copy4 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy4 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy4 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy4 line132

** Testing copy4 line134

Running the tests: copy4 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy4 line138
# Clojure copy4 line139
lein test copy4 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy4 line141
npm install copy4 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy4 line143
lein doo node once copy4 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy4 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy4 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy4 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy4 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy4 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy4 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy4 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy4 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy4 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy4 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy4 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy4 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy4 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy4 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy4 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy4 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy4 line174
lein deps copy4 line175
npm install copy4 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy4 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy4 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy4 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy4 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy4 line183
lein test copy4 line184
lein doo node once copy4 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy4 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy4 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy4 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy4 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy4 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy4 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy4 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy4 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy4 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy4 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy4 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy4 line201
a Lisp before. copy4 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy4 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy4 line206

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy5 line192

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy5 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy6 line6

** What does this project do? copy6 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy6 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy6 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy6 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy6 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy6 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy6 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy6 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy6 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy6 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy6 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy6 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy6 line26
   within Emacs. copy6 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy6 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy6 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy6 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy6 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy6 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy6 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy6 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy6 line36
to share logic between them. copy6 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy6 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy6 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy6 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy6 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy6 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy6 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy6 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy6 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy6 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy6 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy6 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy6 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy6 line51
unambiguous way. copy6 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy6 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy6 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy6 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy6 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy6 line58

** Architecture copy6 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy6 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy6 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy6 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy6 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy6 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy6 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy6 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy6 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy6 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy6 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy6 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy6 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy6 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy6 line78
=org=. copy6 line79

* Project State copy6 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy6 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy6 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy6 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy6 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy6 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy6 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy6 line91
      - [X] Links copy6 line92
      - [X] Text links copy6 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy6 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy6 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy6 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy6 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy6 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy6 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy6 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy6 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy6 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy6 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy6 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy6 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy6 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy6 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy6 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy6 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy6 line112

* Development copy6 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy6 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy6 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy6 line118
suite expectations. copy6 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy6 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy6 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy6 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy6 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy6 line125

** Prerequisites copy6 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy6 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy6 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy6 line132

** Testing copy6 line134

Running the tests: copy6 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy6 line138

lein test copy6 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy6 line141
npm install copy6 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy6 line143
lein doo node once copy6 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy6 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy6 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy6 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy6 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy6 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy6 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy6 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy6 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy6 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy6 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy6 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy6 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy6 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy6 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy6 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy6 line173

lein deps copy6 line175
npm install copy6 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy6 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy6 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy6 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy6 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy6 line183
lein test copy6 line184
lein doo node once copy6 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy6 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy6 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy6 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy6 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy6 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy6 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy6 line195

** Prerequisites copy7 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy7 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy7 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy7 line132

** Testing copy7 line134

Running the tests: copy7 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy7 line138
# Clojure copy7 line139
lein test copy7 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy7 line141
npm install copy7 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy7 line143
lein doo node once copy7 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy7 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy7 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy7 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy7 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy7 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy7 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy7 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy7 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy7 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy7 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy7 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy7 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy7 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy7 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy7 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy7 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy7 line174
lein deps copy7 line175
npm install copy7 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy7 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy7 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy7 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy7 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy7 line183
lein test copy7 line184
lein doo node once copy7 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy7 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy7 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy7 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy7 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy7 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy7 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy7 line195

was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy7 line200

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy8 line6

** What does this project do? copy8 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy8 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy8 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy8 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy8 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy8 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy8 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy8 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy8 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy8 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy8 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy8 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy8 line26
   within Emacs. copy8 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy8 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy8 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy8 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy8 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy8 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy8 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy8 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy8 line36
to share logic between them. copy8 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy8 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy8 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy8 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy8 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy8 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy8 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy8 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy8 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy8 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy8 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy8 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy8 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy8 line51
unambiguous way. copy8 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy8 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy8 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy8 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy8 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy8 line58

** Architecture copy8 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy8 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy8 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy8 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy8 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy8 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy8 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy8 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy8 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy8 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy8 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy8 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy8 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy8 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy8 line78
=org=. copy8 line79

* Project State copy8 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy8 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy8 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy8 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy8 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy8 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy8 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy8 line91
      - [X] Links copy8 line92
      - [X] Text links copy8 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy8 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy8 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy8 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy8 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy8 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy8 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy8 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy8 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy8 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy8 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy8 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy8 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy8 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy8 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy8 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy8 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy8 line112

* Development copy8 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy8 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy8 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy8 line118
suite expectations. copy8 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy8 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy8 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy8 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy8 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy8 line125

      - [X] Headlines copy9 line89

- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy9 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy9 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy9 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy9 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy9 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy9 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy9 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy9 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy9 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy9 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy9 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy9 line112

* Development copy9 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy9 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy9 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy9 line118
suite expectations. copy9 line119

and transform boundaries are documented in copy9 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy9 line123

** Prerequisites copy9 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy9 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy9 line131

** Testing copy9 line134

Running the tests: copy9 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy9 line138

lein test copy9 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy9 line141

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy9 line143
lein doo node once copy9 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy9 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy9 line149

;; discover supported parser start rules copy9 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy9 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy9 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy9 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy9 line158

#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy9 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy9 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy9 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy9 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy9 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy9 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy9 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy9 line174
lein deps copy9 line175
npm install copy9 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy9 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy9 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy9 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy9 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy9 line183
lein test copy9 line184
lein doo node once copy9 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy9 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy9 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy9 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy9 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy9 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy9 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy9 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy10 line6

** What does this project do? copy10 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy10 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy10 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy10 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy10 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy10 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy10 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy10 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy10 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy10 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy10 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy10 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy10 line26
   within Emacs. copy10 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy10 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy10 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy10 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy10 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy10 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy10 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy10 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy10 line36
to share logic between them. copy10 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy10 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy10 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy10 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy10 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy10 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy10 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy10 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy10 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy10 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy10 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy10 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy10 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy10 line51
unambiguous way. copy10 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy10 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy10 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy10 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy10 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy10 line58

** Architecture copy10 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy10 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy10 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy10 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy10 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy10 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy10 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy10 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy10 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy10 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy10 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy10 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy10 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy10 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy10 line78
=org=. copy10 line79

* Project State copy10 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy10 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy10 line84

- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy10 line98

      features that are already supported by the parser. copy10 line100

suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy11 line51
unambiguous way. copy11 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy11 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy11 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy11 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy11 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy11 line58

** Architecture copy11 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy11 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy11 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy11 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy11 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy11 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy11 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy11 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy11 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy11 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy11 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy11 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy11 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy11 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy11 line78
=org=. copy11 line79

* Project State copy11 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy11 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy11 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy11 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy11 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy11 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy11 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy11 line91
      - [X] Links copy11 line92
      - [X] Text links copy11 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy11 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy11 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy11 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy11 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy11 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy11 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy11 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy11 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy11 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy11 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy11 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy11 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy11 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy11 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy11 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy11 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy11 line112

* Development copy11 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy11 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy11 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy11 line118
suite expectations. copy11 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy11 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy11 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy11 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy11 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy11 line125

** Prerequisites copy11 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy11 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy11 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy11 line132

** Testing copy11 line134

Running the tests: copy11 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy11 line138
# Clojure copy11 line139
lein test copy11 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy11 line141
npm install copy11 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy11 line143
lein doo node once copy11 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy11 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy11 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy11 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy11 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy11 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy11 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy11 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy11 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy11 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy11 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy11 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy11 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy11 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy11 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy11 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy11 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy11 line174
lein deps copy11 line175
npm install copy11 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy11 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy11 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy11 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy11 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy11 line183
lein test copy11 line184
lein doo node once copy11 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy11 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy11 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy11 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy11 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy11 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy11 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy11 line195

** What does this project do? copy12 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy12 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy12 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy12 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy12 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy12 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy12 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy12 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy12 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy12 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy12 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy12 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy12 line26
   within Emacs. copy12 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy12 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy12 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy12 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy12 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy12 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy12 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy12 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy12 line36
to share logic between them. copy12 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy12 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy12 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy12 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy12 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy12 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy12 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy12 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy12 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy12 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy12 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy12 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy12 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy12 line51
unambiguous way. copy12 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy12 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy12 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy12 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy12 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy12 line58

** Architecture copy12 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy12 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy12 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy12 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy12 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy12 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy12 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy12 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy12 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy12 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy12 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy12 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy12 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy12 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy12 line78
=org=. copy12 line79

* Project State copy12 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy12 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy12 line84

E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy12 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy12 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy12 line106

e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy12 line108

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy12 line116

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy13 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy13 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy13 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy13 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy13 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy13 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy13 line26
   within Emacs. copy13 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy13 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy13 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy13 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy13 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy13 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy13 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy13 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy13 line36
to share logic between them. copy13 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy13 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy13 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy13 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy13 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy13 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy13 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy13 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy13 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy13 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy13 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy13 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy13 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy13 line51
unambiguous way. copy13 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy13 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy13 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy13 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy13 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy13 line58

** Architecture copy13 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy13 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy13 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy13 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy13 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy13 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy13 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy13 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy13 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy13 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy13 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy13 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy13 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy13 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy13 line78
=org=. copy13 line79

* Project State copy13 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy13 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy13 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy13 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy13 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy13 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy13 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy13 line91
      - [X] Links copy13 line92
      - [X] Text links copy13 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy13 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy13 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy13 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy13 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy13 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy13 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy13 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy13 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy13 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy13 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy13 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy13 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy13 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy13 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy13 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy13 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy13 line112

* Development copy13 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy13 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy13 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy13 line118
suite expectations. copy13 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy13 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy13 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy13 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy13 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy13 line125

** Prerequisites copy13 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy13 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy13 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy13 line132

** Testing copy13 line134

Running the tests: copy13 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy13 line138
# Clojure copy13 line139
lein test copy13 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy13 line141
npm install copy13 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy13 line143
lein doo node once copy13 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy13 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy13 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy13 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy13 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy13 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy13 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy13 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy13 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy13 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy13 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy13 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy13 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy13 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy13 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy13 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy13 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy13 line174
lein deps copy13 line175
npm install copy13 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy13 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy13 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy13 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy13 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy13 line183
lein test copy13 line184
lein doo node once copy13 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy13 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy13 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy13 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy13 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy13 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy13 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy13 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy13 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy13 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy13 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy13 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy13 line201
a Lisp before. copy13 line202
