** What does this project do? copy14 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy14 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy14 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy14 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy14 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy14 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy14 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy14 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy14 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy14 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy14 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy14 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy14 line26
   within Emacs. copy14 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy14 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy14 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy14 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy14 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy14 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy14 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy14 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy14 line36
to share logic between them. copy14 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy14 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy14 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy14 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy14 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy14 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy14 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy14 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy14 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy14 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy14 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy14 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy14 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy14 line51
unambiguous way. copy14 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy14 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy14 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy14 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy14 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy14 line58

- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy14 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy14 line69

reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy14 line78

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy15 line6

** What does this project do? copy15 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy15 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy15 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy15 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy15 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy15 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy15 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy15 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy15 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy15 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy15 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy15 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy15 line26
   within Emacs. copy15 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy15 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy15 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy15 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy15 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy15 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy15 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy15 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy15 line36
to share logic between them. copy15 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy15 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy15 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy15 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy15 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy15 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy15 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy15 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy15 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy15 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy15 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy15 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy15 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy15 line51
unambiguous way. copy15 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy15 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy15 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy15 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy15 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy15 line58

** Architecture copy15 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy15 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy15 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy15 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy15 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy15 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy15 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy15 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy15 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy15 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy15 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy15 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy15 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy15 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy15 line78
=org=. copy15 line79

* Project State copy15 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy15 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy15 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy15 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy15 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy15 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy15 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy15 line91
      - [X] Links copy15 line92
      - [X] Text links copy15 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy15 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy15 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy15 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy15 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy15 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy15 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy15 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy15 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy15 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy15 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy15 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy15 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy15 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy15 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy15 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy15 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy15 line112

* Development copy15 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy15 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy15 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy15 line118
suite expectations. copy15 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy15 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy15 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy15 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy15 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy15 line125

** Prerequisites copy15 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy15 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy15 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy15 line132

** Testing copy15 line134

Running the tests: copy15 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy15 line138
# Clojure copy15 line139
lein test copy15 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy15 line141
npm install copy15 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy15 line143
lein doo node once copy15 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy15 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy15 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy15 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy15 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy15 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy15 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy15 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy15 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy15 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy15 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy15 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy15 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy15 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy15 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy15 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy15 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy15 line174
lein deps copy15 line175
npm install copy15 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy15 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy15 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy15 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy15 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy15 line183
lein test copy15 line184
lein doo node once copy15 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy15 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy15 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy15 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy15 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy15 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy15 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy15 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy15 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy15 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy15 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy15 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy15 line201
a Lisp before. copy15 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy15 line204

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy17 line6

** What does this project do? copy17 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy17 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy17 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy17 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy17 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy17 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy17 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy17 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy17 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy17 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy17 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy17 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy17 line26
   within Emacs. copy17 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy17 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy17 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy17 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy17 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy17 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy17 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy17 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy17 line36
to share logic between them. copy17 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy17 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy17 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy17 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy17 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy17 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy17 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy17 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy17 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy17 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy17 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy17 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy17 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy17 line51
unambiguous way. copy17 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy17 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy17 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy17 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy17 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy17 line58

** Architecture copy17 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy17 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy17 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy17 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy17 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy17 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy17 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy17 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy17 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy17 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy17 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy17 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy17 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy17 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy17 line78
=org=. copy17 line79

* Project State copy17 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy17 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy17 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy17 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy17 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy17 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy17 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy17 line91
      - [X] Links copy17 line92
      - [X] Text links copy17 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy17 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy17 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy17 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy17 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy17 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy17 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy17 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy17 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy17 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy17 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy17 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy17 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy17 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy17 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy17 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy17 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy17 line112

* Development copy17 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy17 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy17 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy17 line118
suite expectations. copy17 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy17 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy17 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy17 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy17 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy17 line125

** Prerequisites copy17 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy17 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy17 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy17 line132

** Testing copy17 line134

Running the tests: copy17 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy17 line138
# Clojure copy17 line139
lein test copy17 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy17 line141
npm install copy17 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy17 line143
lein doo node once copy17 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy17 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy17 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy17 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy17 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy17 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy17 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy17 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy17 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy17 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy17 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy17 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy17 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy17 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy17 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy17 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy17 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy17 line174
lein deps copy17 line175
npm install copy17 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy17 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy17 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy17 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy17 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy17 line183
lein test copy17 line184
lein doo node once copy17 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy17 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy17 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy17 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy17 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy17 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy17 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy17 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy17 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy17 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy17 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy17 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy17 line201
a Lisp before. copy17 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy17 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy17 line206

** What does this project do? copy19 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy19 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy19 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy19 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy19 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy19 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy19 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy19 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy19 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy19 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy19 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy19 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy19 line26
   within Emacs. copy19 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy19 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy19 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy19 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy19 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy19 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy19 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy19 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy19 line36
to share logic between them. copy19 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy19 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy19 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy19 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy19 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy19 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy19 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy19 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy19 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy19 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy19 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy19 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy19 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy19 line51
unambiguous way. copy19 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy19 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy19 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy19 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy19 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy19 line58

** Architecture copy19 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy19 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy19 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy19 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy19 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy19 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy19 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy19 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy19 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy19 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy19 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy19 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy19 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy19 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy19 line78
=org=. copy19 line79

* Project State copy19 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy19 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy19 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy19 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy19 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy19 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy19 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy19 line91
      - [X] Links copy19 line92
      - [X] Text links copy19 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy19 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy19 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy19 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy19 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy19 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy19 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy19 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy19 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy19 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy19 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy19 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy19 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy19 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy19 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy19 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy19 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy19 line112

* Development copy19 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy19 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy19 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy19 line118
suite expectations. copy19 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy19 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy19 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy19 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy19 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy19 line125

** Prerequisites copy19 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy19 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy19 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy19 line132

** Testing copy19 line134

Running the tests: copy19 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy19 line138
# Clojure copy19 line139
lein test copy19 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy19 line141
npm install copy19 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy19 line143
lein doo node once copy19 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy19 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy19 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy19 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy19 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy19 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy19 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy19 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy19 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy19 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy19 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy19 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy19 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy19 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy19 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy19 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy19 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy19 line174
lein deps copy19 line175
npm install copy19 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy19 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy19 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy19 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy19 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy19 line183
lein test copy19 line184
lein doo node once copy19 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy19 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy19 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy19 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy19 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy19 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy19 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy19 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy19 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy19 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy19 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy19 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy19 line201
a Lisp before. copy19 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy19 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy19 line206

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy20 line162

** Contributor Workflow copy20 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy20 line171

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy20 line180

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy20 line192

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy20 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy21 line6

** What does this project do? copy21 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy21 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy21 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy21 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy21 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy21 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy21 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy21 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy21 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy21 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy21 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy21 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy21 line26
   within Emacs. copy21 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy21 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy21 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy21 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy21 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy21 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy21 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy21 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy21 line36
to share logic between them. copy21 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy21 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy21 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy21 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy21 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy21 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy21 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy21 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy21 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy21 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy21 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy21 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy21 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy21 line51
unambiguous way. copy21 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy21 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy21 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy21 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy21 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy21 line58

** Architecture copy21 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy21 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy21 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy21 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy21 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy21 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy21 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy21 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy21 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy21 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy21 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy21 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy21 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy21 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy21 line78
=org=. copy21 line79

* Project State copy21 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy21 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy21 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy21 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy21 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy21 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy21 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy21 line91
      - [X] Links copy21 line92
      - [X] Text links copy21 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy21 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy21 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy21 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy21 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy21 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy21 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy21 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy21 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy21 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy21 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy21 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy21 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy21 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy21 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy21 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy21 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy21 line112

* Development copy21 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy21 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy21 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy21 line118
suite expectations. copy21 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy21 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy21 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy21 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy21 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy21 line125

** Prerequisites copy21 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy21 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy21 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy21 line132

** Testing copy21 line134

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy21 line138

# CLJS (single run) copy21 line141
npm install copy21 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy21 line143
lein doo node once copy21 line144
#+END_SRC

#+END_SRC

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy21 line178

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy21 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy21 line182

#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy21 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy21 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy21 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy21 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy21 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy21 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy21 line195

capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy22 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy22 line112

* Development copy22 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy22 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy22 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy22 line118
suite expectations. copy22 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy22 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy22 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy22 line123

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy22 line162

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy22 line192

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy22 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy23 line6

** What does this project do? copy23 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy23 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy23 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy23 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy23 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy23 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy23 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy23 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy23 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy23 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy23 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy23 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy23 line26
   within Emacs. copy23 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy23 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy23 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy23 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy23 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy23 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy23 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy23 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy23 line36
to share logic between them. copy23 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy23 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy23 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy23 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy23 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy23 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy23 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy23 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy23 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy23 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy23 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy23 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy23 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy23 line51
unambiguous way. copy23 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy23 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy23 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy23 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy23 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy23 line58

** Architecture copy23 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy23 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy23 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy23 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy23 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy23 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy23 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy23 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy23 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy23 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy23 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy23 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy23 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy23 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy23 line78
=org=. copy23 line79

* Project State copy23 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy23 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy23 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy23 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy23 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy23 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy23 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy23 line91
      - [X] Links copy23 line92
      - [X] Text links copy23 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy23 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy23 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy23 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy23 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy23 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy23 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy23 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy23 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy23 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy23 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy23 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy23 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy23 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy23 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy23 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy23 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy23 line112

* Development copy23 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy23 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy23 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy23 line118
suite expectations. copy23 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy23 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy23 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy23 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy23 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy23 line125

* Project State copy24 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy24 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy24 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy24 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy24 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy24 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy24 line90

- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy24 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy24 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy24 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy24 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy24 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy24 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy24 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy24 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy24 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy24 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy24 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy24 line112

* Development copy24 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy24 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy24 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy24 line118
suite expectations. copy24 line119

and transform boundaries are documented in copy24 line122

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy24 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy24 line173

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy24 line180

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy24 line192

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy24 line195

** What does this project do? copy25 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy25 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy25 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy25 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy25 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy25 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy25 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy25 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy25 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy25 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy25 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy25 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy25 line26
   within Emacs. copy25 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy25 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy25 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy25 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy25 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy25 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy25 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy25 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy25 line36
to share logic between them. copy25 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy25 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy25 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy25 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy25 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy25 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy25 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy25 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy25 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy25 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy25 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy25 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy25 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy25 line51
unambiguous way. copy25 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy25 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy25 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy25 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy25 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy25 line58

** Architecture copy25 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy25 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy25 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy25 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy25 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy25 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy25 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy25 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy25 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy25 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy25 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy25 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy25 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy25 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy25 line78
=org=. copy25 line79

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy25 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy25 line84

      features that are already supported by the parser. copy25 line100

*It can already be useful* for you: copy25 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy25 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy25 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy25 line106

parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy25 line109

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy25 line116

syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy26 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy26 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy26 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy26 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy26 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy26 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy26 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy26 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy26 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy26 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy26 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy26 line51
unambiguous way. copy26 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy26 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy26 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy26 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy26 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy26 line58

** Architecture copy26 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy26 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy26 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy26 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy26 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy26 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy26 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy26 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy26 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy26 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy26 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy26 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy26 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy26 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy26 line78
=org=. copy26 line79

* Project State copy26 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy26 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy26 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy26 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy26 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy26 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy26 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy26 line91
      - [X] Links copy26 line92
      - [X] Text links copy26 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy26 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy26 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy26 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy26 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy26 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy26 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy26 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy26 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy26 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy26 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy26 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy26 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy26 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy26 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy26 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy26 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy26 line112

* Development copy26 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy26 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy26 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy26 line118
suite expectations. copy26 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy26 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy26 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy26 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy26 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy26 line125

** Prerequisites copy26 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy26 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy26 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy26 line132

** Testing copy26 line134

Running the tests: copy26 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy26 line138
# Clojure copy26 line139
lein test copy26 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy26 line141
npm install copy26 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy26 line143
lein doo node once copy26 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy26 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy26 line149

;; discover supported parser start rules copy26 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy26 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy26 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy26 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy26 line158

#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy26 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy26 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy26 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy26 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy26 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy26 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy26 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy26 line174
lein deps copy26 line175
npm install copy26 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy26 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy26 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy26 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy26 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy26 line183
lein test copy26 line184
lein doo node once copy26 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy26 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy26 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy26 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy26 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy26 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy26 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy26 line195

how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy26 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy26 line199

** What does this project do? copy27 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy27 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy27 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy27 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy27 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy27 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy27 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy27 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy27 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy27 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy27 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy27 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy27 line26
   within Emacs. copy27 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy27 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy27 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy27 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy27 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy27 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy27 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy27 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy27 line36
to share logic between them. copy27 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy27 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy27 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy27 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy27 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy27 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy27 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy27 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy27 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy27 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy27 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy27 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy27 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy27 line51
unambiguous way. copy27 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy27 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy27 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy27 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy27 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy27 line58

** Architecture copy27 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy27 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy27 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy27 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy27 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy27 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy27 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy27 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy27 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy27 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy27 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy27 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy27 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy27 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy27 line78
=org=. copy27 line79

because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy27 line84

** What does this project do? copy28 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy28 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy28 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy28 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy28 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy28 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy28 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy28 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy28 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy28 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy28 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy28 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy28 line26
   within Emacs. copy28 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy28 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy28 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy28 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy28 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy28 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy28 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy28 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy28 line36
to share logic between them. copy28 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy28 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy28 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy28 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy28 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy28 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy28 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy28 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy28 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy28 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy28 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy28 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy28 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy28 line51
unambiguous way. copy28 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy28 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy28 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy28 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy28 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy28 line58

** Architecture copy28 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy28 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy28 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy28 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy28 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy28 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy28 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy28 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy28 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy28 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy28 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy28 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy28 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy28 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy28 line78
=org=. copy28 line79

* Project State copy28 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy28 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy28 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy28 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy28 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy28 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy28 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy28 line91
      - [X] Links copy28 line92
      - [X] Text links copy28 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy28 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy28 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy28 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy28 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy28 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy28 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy28 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy28 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy28 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy28 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy28 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy28 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy28 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy28 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy28 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy28 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy28 line112

* Development copy28 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy28 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy28 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy28 line118
suite expectations. copy28 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy28 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy28 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy28 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy28 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy28 line125

** Prerequisites copy28 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy28 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy28 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy28 line132

** Testing copy28 line134

Running the tests: copy28 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy28 line138
# Clojure copy28 line139
lein test copy28 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy28 line141
npm install copy28 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy28 line143
lein doo node once copy28 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy28 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy28 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy28 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy28 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy28 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy28 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy28 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy28 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy28 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy28 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy28 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy28 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy28 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy28 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy28 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy28 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy28 line174
lein deps copy28 line175
npm install copy28 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy28 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy28 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy28 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy28 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy28 line183
lein test copy28 line184
lein doo node once copy28 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy28 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy28 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy28 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy28 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy28 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy28 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy28 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy28 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy28 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy28 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy28 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy28 line201
a Lisp before. copy28 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy28 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy28 line206

* Usage copy28 line225
  :PROPERTIES:
  :CUSTOM_ID: usage copy28 line227

At the moment, you can run =org-parser= from Clojure, ClojureScript, copy28 line230
or Java. Other targets which are hosted on the JVM or on JavaScript copy28 line231
are possible. copy28 line232

** Clojure Library copy28 line234

 #+BEGIN_SRC clojure :exports both copy28 line236
   (ns hello-world.core copy28 line237
     (:require [org-parser.parser :refer [parse]] copy28 line238
               [org-parser.core :refer [read-str write-str]])) copy28 line239

   (prn (parse "* Headline")) copy28 line241
   (prn (read-str "* Headline")) copy28 line242
   (println (write-str (read-str "* Headline"))) copy28 line243
 #+END_SRC

 | "* Headline\n"                                                                                |
