Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy30 line6

** What does this project do? copy30 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy30 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy30 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy30 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy30 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy30 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy30 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy30 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy30 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy30 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy30 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy30 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy30 line26
   within Emacs. copy30 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy30 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy30 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy30 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy30 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy30 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy30 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy30 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy30 line36
to share logic between them. copy30 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy30 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy30 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy30 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy30 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy30 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy30 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy30 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy30 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy30 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy30 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy30 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy30 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy30 line51
unambiguous way. copy30 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy30 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy30 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy30 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy30 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy30 line58

** Architecture copy30 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy30 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy30 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy30 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy30 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy30 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy30 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy30 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy30 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy30 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy30 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy30 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy30 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy30 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy30 line78
=org=. copy30 line79

* Project State copy30 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy30 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy30 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy30 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy30 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy30 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy30 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy30 line91
      - [X] Links copy30 line92
      - [X] Text links copy30 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy30 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy30 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy30 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy30 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy30 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy30 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy30 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy30 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy30 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy30 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy30 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy30 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy30 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy30 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy30 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy30 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy30 line112

* Development copy30 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy30 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy30 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy30 line118
suite expectations. copy30 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy30 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy30 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy30 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy30 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy30 line125

** Prerequisites copy30 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy30 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy30 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy30 line132

** Testing copy30 line134

Running the tests: copy30 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy30 line138
# Clojure copy30 line139
lein test copy30 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy30 line141
npm install copy30 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy30 line143
lein doo node once copy30 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy30 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy30 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy30 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy30 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy30 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy30 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy30 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy30 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy30 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy30 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy30 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy30 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy30 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy30 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy30 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy30 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy30 line174
lein deps copy30 line175
npm install copy30 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy30 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy30 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy30 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy30 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy30 line183
lein test copy30 line184
lein doo node once copy30 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy30 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy30 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy30 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy30 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy30 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy30 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy30 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy30 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy30 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy30 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy30 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy30 line201
a Lisp before. copy30 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy30 line204

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy32 line6

** What does this project do? copy32 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy32 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy32 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy32 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy32 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy32 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy32 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy32 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy32 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy32 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy32 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy32 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy32 line26
   within Emacs. copy32 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy32 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy32 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy32 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy32 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy32 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy32 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy32 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy32 line36
to share logic between them. copy32 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy32 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy32 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy32 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy32 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy32 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy32 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy32 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy32 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy32 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy32 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy32 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy32 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy32 line51
unambiguous way. copy32 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy32 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy32 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy32 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy32 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy32 line58

** Architecture copy32 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy32 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy32 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy32 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy32 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy32 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy32 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy32 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy32 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy32 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy32 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy32 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy32 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy32 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy32 line78
=org=. copy32 line79

* Project State copy32 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy32 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy32 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy32 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy32 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy32 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy32 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy32 line91
      - [X] Links copy32 line92
      - [X] Text links copy32 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy32 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy32 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy32 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy32 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy32 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy32 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy32 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy32 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy32 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy32 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy32 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy32 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy32 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy32 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy32 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy32 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy32 line112

* Development copy32 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy32 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy32 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy32 line118
suite expectations. copy32 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy32 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy32 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy32 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy32 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy32 line125

** Prerequisites copy32 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy32 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy32 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy32 line132

** Testing copy32 line134

Running the tests: copy32 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy32 line138
# Clojure copy32 line139
lein test copy32 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy32 line141
npm install copy32 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy32 line143
lein doo node once copy32 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy32 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy32 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy32 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy32 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy32 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy32 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy32 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy32 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy32 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy32 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy32 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy32 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy32 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy32 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy32 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy32 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy32 line174
lein deps copy32 line175
npm install copy32 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy32 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy32 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy32 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy32 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy32 line183
lein test copy32 line184
lein doo node once copy32 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy32 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy32 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy32 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy32 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy32 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy32 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy32 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy32 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy32 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy32 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy32 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy32 line201
a Lisp before. copy32 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy32 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy32 line206

** What does this project do? copy34 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy34 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy34 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy34 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy34 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy34 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy34 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy34 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy34 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy34 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy34 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy34 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy34 line26
   within Emacs. copy34 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy34 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy34 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy34 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy34 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy34 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy34 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy34 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy34 line36
to share logic between them. copy34 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy34 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy34 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy34 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy34 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy34 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy34 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy34 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy34 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy34 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy34 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy34 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy34 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy34 line51
unambiguous way. copy34 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy34 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy34 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy34 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy34 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy34 line58

** Architecture copy34 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy34 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy34 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy34 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy34 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy34 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy34 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy34 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy34 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy34 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy34 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy34 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy34 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy34 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy34 line78
=org=. copy34 line79

* Project State copy34 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy34 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy34 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy34 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy34 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy34 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy34 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy34 line91
      - [X] Links copy34 line92
      - [X] Text links copy34 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy34 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy34 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy34 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy34 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy34 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy34 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy34 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy34 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy34 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy34 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy34 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy34 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy34 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy34 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy34 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy34 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy34 line112

* Development copy34 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy34 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy34 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy34 line118
suite expectations. copy34 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy34 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy34 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy34 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy34 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy34 line125

** Prerequisites copy34 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy34 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy34 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy34 line132

** Testing copy34 line134

Running the tests: copy34 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy34 line138
# Clojure copy34 line139
lein test copy34 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy34 line141
npm install copy34 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy34 line143
lein doo node once copy34 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy34 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy34 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy34 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy34 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy34 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy34 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy34 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy34 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy34 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy34 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy34 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy34 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy34 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy34 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy34 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy34 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy34 line174
lein deps copy34 line175
npm install copy34 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy34 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy34 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy34 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy34 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy34 line183
lein test copy34 line184
lein doo node once copy34 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy34 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy34 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy34 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy34 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy34 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy34 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy34 line195

** Contributor Workflow copy35 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy35 line171

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy35 line180

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy35 line192

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy35 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy36 line6

** What does this project do? copy36 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy36 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy36 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy36 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy36 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy36 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy36 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy36 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy36 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy36 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy36 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy36 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy36 line26
   within Emacs. copy36 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy36 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy36 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy36 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy36 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy36 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy36 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy36 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy36 line36
to share logic between them. copy36 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy36 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy36 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy36 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy36 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy36 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy36 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy36 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy36 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy36 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy36 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy36 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy36 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy36 line51
unambiguous way. copy36 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy36 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy36 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy36 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy36 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy36 line58

** Architecture copy36 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy36 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy36 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy36 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy36 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy36 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy36 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy36 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy36 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy36 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy36 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy36 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy36 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy36 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy36 line78
=org=. copy36 line79

* Project State copy36 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy36 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy36 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy36 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy36 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy36 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy36 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy36 line91
      - [X] Links copy36 line92
      - [X] Text links copy36 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy36 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy36 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy36 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy36 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy36 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy36 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy36 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy36 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy36 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy36 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy36 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy36 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy36 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy36 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy36 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy36 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy36 line112

* Development copy36 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy36 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy36 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy36 line118
suite expectations. copy36 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy36 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy36 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy36 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy36 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy36 line125

** Prerequisites copy36 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy36 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy36 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy36 line132

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy36 line180

Common troubleshooting: copy36 line188

  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy36 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy36 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy36 line193

** What does this project do? copy38 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy38 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy38 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy38 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy38 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy38 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy38 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy38 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy38 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy38 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy38 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy38 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy38 line26
   within Emacs. copy38 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy38 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy38 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy38 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy38 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy38 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy38 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy38 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy38 line36
to share logic between them. copy38 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy38 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy38 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy38 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy38 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy38 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy38 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy38 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy38 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy38 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy38 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy38 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy38 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy38 line51
unambiguous way. copy38 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy38 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy38 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy38 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy38 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy38 line58

** Architecture copy38 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy38 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy38 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy38 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy38 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy38 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy38 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy38 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy38 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy38 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy38 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy38 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy38 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy38 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy38 line78
=org=. copy38 line79

* Project State copy38 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy38 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy38 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy38 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy38 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy38 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy38 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy38 line91
      - [X] Links copy38 line92
      - [X] Text links copy38 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy38 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy38 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy38 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy38 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy38 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy38 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy38 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy38 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy38 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy38 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy38 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy38 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy38 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy38 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy38 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy38 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy38 line112

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy38 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy38 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy38 line118
suite expectations. copy38 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy38 line121

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy39 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy39 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy39 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy39 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy39 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy39 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy39 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy39 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy39 line78
=org=. copy39 line79

* Project State copy39 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy39 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy39 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy39 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy39 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy39 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy39 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy39 line91
      - [X] Links copy39 line92
      - [X] Text links copy39 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy39 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy39 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy39 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy39 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy39 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy39 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy39 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy39 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy39 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy39 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy39 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy39 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy39 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy39 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy39 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy39 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy39 line112

* Development copy39 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy39 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy39 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy39 line118
suite expectations. copy39 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy39 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy39 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy39 line123

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy39 line131

** Testing copy39 line134

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy39 line162

** Contributor Workflow copy39 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy39 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy39 line173

lein deps copy39 line175
npm install copy39 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy39 line178

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy39 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy39 line182

#+END_SRC

  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy39 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy39 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy39 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy39 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy39 line195

** What does this project do? copy40 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy40 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy40 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy40 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy40 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy40 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy40 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy40 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy40 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy40 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy40 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy40 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy40 line26
   within Emacs. copy40 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy40 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy40 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy40 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy40 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy40 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy40 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy40 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy40 line36
to share logic between them. copy40 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy40 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy40 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy40 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy40 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy40 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy40 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy40 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy40 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy40 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy40 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy40 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy40 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy40 line51
unambiguous way. copy40 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy40 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy40 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy40 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy40 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy40 line58

** Architecture copy40 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy40 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy40 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy40 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy40 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy40 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy40 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy40 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy40 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy40 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy40 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy40 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy40 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy40 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy40 line78
=org=. copy40 line79

* Project State copy40 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy40 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy40 line84

- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy40 line98

      features that are already supported by the parser. copy40 line100

*It can already be useful* for you: copy40 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy40 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy40 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy40 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy40 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy40 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy40 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy40 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy40 line112

* Development copy40 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy40 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy40 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy40 line118
suite expectations. copy40 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy40 line121

org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy41 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy41 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy41 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy41 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy41 line36
to share logic between them. copy41 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy41 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy41 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy41 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy41 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy41 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy41 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy41 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy41 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy41 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy41 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy41 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy41 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy41 line51
unambiguous way. copy41 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy41 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy41 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy41 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy41 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy41 line58

** Architecture copy41 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy41 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy41 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy41 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy41 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy41 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy41 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy41 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy41 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy41 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy41 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy41 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy41 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy41 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy41 line78
=org=. copy41 line79

* Project State copy41 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy41 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy41 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy41 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy41 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy41 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy41 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy41 line91
      - [X] Links copy41 line92
      - [X] Text links copy41 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy41 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy41 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy41 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy41 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy41 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy41 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy41 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy41 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy41 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy41 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy41 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy41 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy41 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy41 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy41 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy41 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy41 line112

* Development copy41 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy41 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy41 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy41 line118
suite expectations. copy41 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy41 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy41 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy41 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy41 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy41 line125

** Prerequisites copy41 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy41 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy41 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy41 line132

** Testing copy41 line134

Running the tests: copy41 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy41 line138
# Clojure copy41 line139
lein test copy41 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy41 line141
npm install copy41 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy41 line143
lein doo node once copy41 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy41 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy41 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy41 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy41 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy41 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy41 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy41 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy41 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy41 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy41 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy41 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy41 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy41 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy41 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy41 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy41 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy41 line174
lein deps copy41 line175
npm install copy41 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy41 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy41 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy41 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy41 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy41 line183
lein test copy41 line184
lein doo node once copy41 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy41 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy41 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy41 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy41 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy41 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy41 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy41 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy41 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy41 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy41 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy41 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy41 line201
a Lisp before. copy41 line202

** What does this project do? copy42 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy42 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy42 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy42 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy42 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy42 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy42 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy42 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy42 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy42 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy42 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy42 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy42 line26
   within Emacs. copy42 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy42 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy42 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy42 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy42 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy42 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy42 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy42 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy42 line36
to share logic between them. copy42 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy42 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy42 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy42 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy42 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy42 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy42 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy42 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy42 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy42 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy42 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy42 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy42 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy42 line51
unambiguous way. copy42 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy42 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy42 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy42 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy42 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy42 line58

** Architecture copy42 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy42 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy42 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy42 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy42 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy42 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy42 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy42 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy42 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy42 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy42 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy42 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy42 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy42 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy42 line78
=org=. copy42 line79

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy42 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy42 line84

*It can already be useful* for you: copy42 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy42 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy42 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy42 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy42 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy42 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy42 line109

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy42 line112

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy42 line116

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy43 line6

** What does this project do? copy43 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy43 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy43 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy43 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy43 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy43 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy43 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy43 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy43 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy43 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy43 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy43 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy43 line26
   within Emacs. copy43 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy43 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy43 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy43 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy43 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy43 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy43 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy43 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy43 line36
to share logic between them. copy43 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy43 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy43 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy43 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy43 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy43 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy43 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy43 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy43 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy43 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy43 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy43 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy43 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy43 line51
unambiguous way. copy43 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy43 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy43 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy43 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy43 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy43 line58

** Architecture copy43 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy43 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy43 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy43 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy43 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy43 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy43 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy43 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy43 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy43 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy43 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy43 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy43 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy43 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy43 line78
=org=. copy43 line79

* Project State copy43 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy43 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy43 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy43 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy43 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy43 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy43 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy43 line91
      - [X] Links copy43 line92
      - [X] Text links copy43 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy43 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy43 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy43 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy43 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy43 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy43 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy43 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy43 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy43 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy43 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy43 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy43 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy43 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy43 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy43 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy43 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy43 line112

* Development copy43 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy43 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy43 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy43 line118
suite expectations. copy43 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy43 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy43 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy43 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy43 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy43 line125

** Prerequisites copy43 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy43 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy43 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy43 line132

** Testing copy43 line134

Running the tests: copy43 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy43 line138
# Clojure copy43 line139
lein test copy43 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy43 line141
npm install copy43 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy43 line143
lein doo node once copy43 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy43 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy43 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy43 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy43 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy43 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy43 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy43 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy43 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy43 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy43 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy43 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy43 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy43 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy43 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy43 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy43 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy43 line174
lein deps copy43 line175
npm install copy43 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy43 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy43 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy43 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy43 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy43 line183
lein test copy43 line184
lein doo node once copy43 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy43 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy43 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy43 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy43 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy43 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy43 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy43 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy43 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy43 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy43 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy43 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy43 line201
a Lisp before. copy43 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy43 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy43 line206

* Release and Dependency Information copy43 line208

* Usage copy43 line225
  :PROPERTIES:
  :CUSTOM_ID: usage copy43 line227
  :END:

At the moment, you can run =org-parser= from Clojure, ClojureScript, copy43 line230
or Java. Other targets which are hosted on the JVM or on JavaScript copy43 line231
are possible. copy43 line232

** Clojure Library copy43 line234

 #+BEGIN_SRC clojure :exports both copy43 line236
   (ns hello-world.core copy43 line237
     (:require [org-parser.parser :refer [parse]] copy43 line238
               [org-parser.core :refer [read-str write-str]])) copy43 line239

   (prn (parse "* Headline")) copy43 line241
   (prn (read-str "* Headline")) copy43 line242
   (println (write-str (read-str "* Headline"))) copy43 line243
 #+END_SRC

 #+RESULTS: copy43 line246
 | [:S [:headline [:stars "*"] [:text [:text-normal "Headline"]]]]                               |
 | {:headlines [{:headline {:level 1, :title [[:text-normal "Headline"]], :planning [], :tags []}}]} |
 | "* Headline\n"                                                                                |

Run =lein run file.org=, for example: copy43 line253

  lein run test/org_parser/fixtures/schedule_with_repeater.org copy43 line256
#+end_src copy43 line257
