Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy45 line6

** What does this project do? copy45 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy45 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy45 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy45 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy45 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy45 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy45 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy45 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy45 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy45 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy45 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy45 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy45 line26
   within Emacs. copy45 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy45 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy45 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy45 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy45 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy45 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy45 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy45 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy45 line36
to share logic between them. copy45 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy45 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy45 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy45 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy45 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy45 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy45 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy45 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy45 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy45 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy45 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy45 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy45 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy45 line51
unambiguous way. copy45 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy45 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy45 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy45 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy45 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy45 line58

** Architecture copy45 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy45 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy45 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy45 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy45 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy45 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy45 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy45 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy45 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy45 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy45 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy45 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy45 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy45 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy45 line78
=org=. copy45 line79

* Project State copy45 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy45 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy45 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy45 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy45 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy45 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy45 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy45 line91
      - [X] Links copy45 line92
      - [X] Text links copy45 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy45 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy45 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy45 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy45 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy45 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy45 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy45 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy45 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy45 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy45 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy45 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy45 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy45 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy45 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy45 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy45 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy45 line112

* Development copy45 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy45 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy45 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy45 line118
suite expectations. copy45 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy45 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy45 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy45 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy45 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy45 line125

** Prerequisites copy45 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy45 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy45 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy45 line132

** Testing copy45 line134

Running the tests: copy45 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy45 line138
# Clojure copy45 line139
lein test copy45 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy45 line141
npm install copy45 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy45 line143
lein doo node once copy45 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy45 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy45 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy45 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy45 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy45 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy45 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy45 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy45 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy45 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy45 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy45 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy45 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy45 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy45 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy45 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy45 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy45 line174
lein deps copy45 line175
npm install copy45 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy45 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy45 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy45 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy45 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy45 line183
lein test copy45 line184
lein doo node once copy45 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy45 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy45 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy45 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy45 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy45 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy45 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy45 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy45 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy45 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy45 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy45 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy45 line201
a Lisp before. copy45 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy45 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy45 line206

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy47 line6

** What does this project do? copy47 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy47 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy47 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy47 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy47 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy47 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy47 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy47 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy47 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy47 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy47 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy47 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy47 line26
   within Emacs. copy47 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy47 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy47 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy47 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy47 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy47 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy47 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy47 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy47 line36
to share logic between them. copy47 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy47 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy47 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy47 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy47 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy47 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy47 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy47 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy47 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy47 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy47 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy47 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy47 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy47 line51
unambiguous way. copy47 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy47 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy47 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy47 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy47 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy47 line58

** Architecture copy47 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy47 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy47 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy47 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy47 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy47 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy47 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy47 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy47 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy47 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy47 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy47 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy47 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy47 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy47 line78
=org=. copy47 line79

* Project State copy47 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy47 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy47 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy47 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy47 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy47 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy47 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy47 line91
      - [X] Links copy47 line92
      - [X] Text links copy47 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy47 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy47 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy47 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy47 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy47 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy47 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy47 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy47 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy47 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy47 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy47 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy47 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy47 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy47 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy47 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy47 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy47 line112

* Development copy47 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy47 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy47 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy47 line118
suite expectations. copy47 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy47 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy47 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy47 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy47 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy47 line125

** Prerequisites copy47 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy47 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy47 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy47 line132

** Testing copy47 line134

Running the tests: copy47 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy47 line138
# Clojure copy47 line139
lein test copy47 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy47 line141
npm install copy47 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy47 line143
lein doo node once copy47 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy47 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy47 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy47 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy47 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy47 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy47 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy47 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy47 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy47 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy47 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy47 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy47 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy47 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy47 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy47 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy47 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy47 line174
lein deps copy47 line175
npm install copy47 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy47 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy47 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy47 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy47 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy47 line183
lein test copy47 line184
lein doo node once copy47 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy47 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy47 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy47 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy47 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy47 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy47 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy47 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy47 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy47 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy47 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy47 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy47 line201
a Lisp before. copy47 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy47 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy47 line206

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy48 line180

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy48 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy48 line193

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy48 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy49 line6

** What does this project do? copy49 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy49 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy49 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy49 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy49 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy49 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy49 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy49 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy49 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy49 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy49 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy49 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy49 line26
   within Emacs. copy49 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy49 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy49 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy49 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy49 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy49 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy49 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy49 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy49 line36
to share logic between them. copy49 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy49 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy49 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy49 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy49 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy49 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy49 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy49 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy49 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy49 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy49 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy49 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy49 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy49 line51
unambiguous way. copy49 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy49 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy49 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy49 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy49 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy49 line58

** Architecture copy49 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy49 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy49 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy49 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy49 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy49 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy49 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy49 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy49 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy49 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy49 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy49 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy49 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy49 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy49 line78
=org=. copy49 line79

* Project State copy49 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy49 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy49 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy49 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy49 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy49 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy49 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy49 line91
      - [X] Links copy49 line92
      - [X] Text links copy49 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy49 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy49 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy49 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy49 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy49 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy49 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy49 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy49 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy49 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy49 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy49 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy49 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy49 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy49 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy49 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy49 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy49 line112

* Development copy49 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy49 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy49 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy49 line118
suite expectations. copy49 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy49 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy49 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy49 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy49 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy49 line125

** Prerequisites copy49 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy49 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy49 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy49 line132

** Testing copy49 line134

Running the tests: copy49 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy49 line138

lein test copy49 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy49 line141
npm install copy49 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy49 line143
lein doo node once copy49 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy49 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy49 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy49 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy49 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy49 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy49 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy49 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy49 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy49 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy49 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy49 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy49 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy49 line166
#+END_SRC

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy49 line171

lein deps copy49 line175
npm install copy49 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy49 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy49 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy49 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy49 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy49 line183
lein test copy49 line184
lein doo node once copy49 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy49 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy49 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy49 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy49 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy49 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy49 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy49 line195

** Prerequisites copy50 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy50 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy50 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy50 line132

** Testing copy50 line134

Running the tests: copy50 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy50 line138
# Clojure copy50 line139
lein test copy50 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy50 line141
npm install copy50 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy50 line143
lein doo node once copy50 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy50 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy50 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy50 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy50 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy50 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy50 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy50 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy50 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy50 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy50 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy50 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy50 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy50 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy50 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy50 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy50 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy50 line174
lein deps copy50 line175
npm install copy50 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy50 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy50 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy50 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy50 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy50 line183
lein test copy50 line184
lein doo node once copy50 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy50 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy50 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy50 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy50 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy50 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy50 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy50 line195

** What does this project do? copy51 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy51 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy51 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy51 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy51 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy51 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy51 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy51 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy51 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy51 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy51 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy51 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy51 line26
   within Emacs. copy51 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy51 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy51 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy51 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy51 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy51 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy51 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy51 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy51 line36
to share logic between them. copy51 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy51 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy51 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy51 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy51 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy51 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy51 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy51 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy51 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy51 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy51 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy51 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy51 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy51 line51
unambiguous way. copy51 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy51 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy51 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy51 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy51 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy51 line58

** Architecture copy51 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy51 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy51 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy51 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy51 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy51 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy51 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy51 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy51 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy51 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy51 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy51 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy51 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy51 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy51 line78
=org=. copy51 line79

* Project State copy51 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy51 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy51 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy51 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy51 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy51 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy51 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy51 line91
      - [X] Links copy51 line92
      - [X] Text links copy51 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy51 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy51 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy51 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy51 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy51 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy51 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy51 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy51 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy51 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy51 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy51 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy51 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy51 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy51 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy51 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy51 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy51 line112

* Development copy51 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy51 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy51 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy51 line118
suite expectations. copy51 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy51 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy51 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy51 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy51 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy51 line125

      features that are already supported by the parser. copy52 line100

*It can already be useful* for you: copy52 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy52 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy52 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy52 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy52 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy52 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy52 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy52 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy52 line112

* Development copy52 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy52 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy52 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy52 line118
suite expectations. copy52 line119

and transform boundaries are documented in copy52 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy52 line123

** Testing copy52 line134

Running the tests: copy52 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy52 line138

lein test copy52 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy52 line141

;; discover supported parser start rules copy52 line152

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy52 line155

;; parse specific start rule copy52 line158

#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy52 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy52 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy52 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy52 line166

** Contributor Workflow copy52 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy52 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy52 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy52 line174
lein deps copy52 line175
npm install copy52 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy52 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy52 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy52 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy52 line182

lein test copy52 line184
lein doo node once copy52 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy52 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy52 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy52 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy52 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy52 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy52 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy52 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy53 line6

** What does this project do? copy53 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy53 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy53 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy53 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy53 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy53 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy53 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy53 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy53 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy53 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy53 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy53 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy53 line26
   within Emacs. copy53 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy53 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy53 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy53 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy53 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy53 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy53 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy53 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy53 line36
to share logic between them. copy53 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy53 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy53 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy53 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy53 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy53 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy53 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy53 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy53 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy53 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy53 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy53 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy53 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy53 line51
unambiguous way. copy53 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy53 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy53 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy53 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy53 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy53 line58

** Architecture copy53 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy53 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy53 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy53 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy53 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy53 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy53 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy53 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy53 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy53 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy53 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy53 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy53 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy53 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy53 line78
=org=. copy53 line79

* Project State copy53 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy53 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy53 line84

suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy54 line51
unambiguous way. copy54 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy54 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy54 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy54 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy54 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy54 line58

** Architecture copy54 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy54 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy54 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy54 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy54 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy54 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy54 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy54 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy54 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy54 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy54 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy54 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy54 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy54 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy54 line78
=org=. copy54 line79

* Project State copy54 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy54 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy54 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy54 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy54 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy54 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy54 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy54 line91
      - [X] Links copy54 line92
      - [X] Text links copy54 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy54 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy54 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy54 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy54 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy54 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy54 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy54 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy54 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy54 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy54 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy54 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy54 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy54 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy54 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy54 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy54 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy54 line112

* Development copy54 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy54 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy54 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy54 line118
suite expectations. copy54 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy54 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy54 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy54 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy54 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy54 line125

** Prerequisites copy54 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy54 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy54 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy54 line132

** Testing copy54 line134

Running the tests: copy54 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy54 line138

lein test copy54 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy54 line141
npm install copy54 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy54 line143
lein doo node once copy54 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy54 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy54 line149

;; discover supported parser start rules copy54 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy54 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy54 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy54 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy54 line158

#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy54 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy54 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy54 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy54 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy54 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy54 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy54 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy54 line174
lein deps copy54 line175
npm install copy54 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy54 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy54 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy54 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy54 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy54 line183
lein test copy54 line184
lein doo node once copy54 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy54 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy54 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy54 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy54 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy54 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy54 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy54 line195

** What does this project do? copy55 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy55 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy55 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy55 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy55 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy55 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy55 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy55 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy55 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy55 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy55 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy55 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy55 line26
   within Emacs. copy55 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy55 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy55 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy55 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy55 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy55 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy55 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy55 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy55 line36
to share logic between them. copy55 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy55 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy55 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy55 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy55 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy55 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy55 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy55 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy55 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy55 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy55 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy55 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy55 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy55 line51
unambiguous way. copy55 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy55 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy55 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy55 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy55 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy55 line58

** Architecture copy55 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy55 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy55 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy55 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy55 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy55 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy55 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy55 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy55 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy55 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy55 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy55 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy55 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy55 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy55 line78
=org=. copy55 line79

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy55 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy55 line84

E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy55 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy55 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy55 line106

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy56 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy56 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy56 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy56 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy56 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy56 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy56 line26
   within Emacs. copy56 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy56 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy56 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy56 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy56 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy56 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy56 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy56 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy56 line36
to share logic between them. copy56 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy56 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy56 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy56 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy56 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy56 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy56 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy56 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy56 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy56 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy56 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy56 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy56 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy56 line51
unambiguous way. copy56 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy56 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy56 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy56 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy56 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy56 line58

** Architecture copy56 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy56 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy56 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy56 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy56 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy56 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy56 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy56 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy56 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy56 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy56 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy56 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy56 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy56 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy56 line78
=org=. copy56 line79

* Project State copy56 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy56 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy56 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy56 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy56 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy56 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy56 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy56 line91
      - [X] Links copy56 line92
      - [X] Text links copy56 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy56 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy56 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy56 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy56 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy56 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy56 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy56 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy56 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy56 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy56 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy56 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy56 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy56 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy56 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy56 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy56 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy56 line112

* Development copy56 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy56 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy56 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy56 line118
suite expectations. copy56 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy56 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy56 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy56 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy56 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy56 line125

** Prerequisites copy56 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy56 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy56 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy56 line132

** Testing copy56 line134

Running the tests: copy56 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy56 line138
# Clojure copy56 line139
lein test copy56 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy56 line141
npm install copy56 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy56 line143
lein doo node once copy56 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy56 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy56 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy56 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy56 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy56 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy56 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy56 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy56 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy56 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy56 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy56 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy56 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy56 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy56 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy56 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy56 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy56 line174
lein deps copy56 line175
npm install copy56 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy56 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy56 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy56 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy56 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy56 line183
lein test copy56 line184
lein doo node once copy56 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy56 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy56 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy56 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy56 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy56 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy56 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy56 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy56 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy56 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy56 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy56 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy56 line201
a Lisp before. copy56 line202

** What does this project do? copy57 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy57 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy57 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy57 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy57 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy57 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy57 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy57 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy57 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy57 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy57 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy57 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy57 line26
   within Emacs. copy57 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy57 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy57 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy57 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy57 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy57 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy57 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy57 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy57 line36
to share logic between them. copy57 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy57 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy57 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy57 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy57 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy57 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy57 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy57 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy57 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy57 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy57 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy57 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy57 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy57 line51
unambiguous way. copy57 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy57 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy57 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy57 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy57 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy57 line58

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy58 line6

** What does this project do? copy58 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy58 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy58 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy58 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy58 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy58 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy58 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy58 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy58 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy58 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy58 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy58 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy58 line26
   within Emacs. copy58 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy58 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy58 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy58 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy58 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy58 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy58 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy58 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy58 line36
to share logic between them. copy58 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy58 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy58 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy58 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy58 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy58 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy58 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy58 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy58 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy58 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy58 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy58 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy58 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy58 line51
unambiguous way. copy58 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy58 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy58 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy58 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy58 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy58 line58

** Architecture copy58 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy58 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy58 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy58 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy58 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy58 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy58 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy58 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy58 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy58 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy58 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy58 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy58 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy58 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy58 line78
=org=. copy58 line79

* Project State copy58 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy58 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy58 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy58 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy58 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy58 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy58 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy58 line91
      - [X] Links copy58 line92
      - [X] Text links copy58 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy58 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy58 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy58 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy58 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy58 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy58 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy58 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy58 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy58 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy58 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy58 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy58 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy58 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy58 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy58 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy58 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy58 line112

* Development copy58 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy58 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy58 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy58 line118
suite expectations. copy58 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy58 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy58 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy58 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy58 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy58 line125

** Prerequisites copy58 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy58 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy58 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy58 line132

** Testing copy58 line134

Running the tests: copy58 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy58 line138
# Clojure copy58 line139
lein test copy58 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy58 line141
npm install copy58 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy58 line143
lein doo node once copy58 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy58 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy58 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy58 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy58 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy58 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy58 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy58 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy58 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy58 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy58 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy58 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy58 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy58 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy58 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy58 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy58 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy58 line174
lein deps copy58 line175
npm install copy58 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy58 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy58 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy58 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy58 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy58 line183
lein test copy58 line184
lein doo node once copy58 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy58 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy58 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy58 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy58 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy58 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy58 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy58 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy58 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy58 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy58 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy58 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy58 line201
a Lisp before. copy58 line202
