Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy60 line6

** What does this project do? copy60 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy60 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy60 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy60 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy60 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy60 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy60 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy60 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy60 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy60 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy60 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy60 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy60 line26
   within Emacs. copy60 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy60 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy60 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy60 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy60 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy60 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy60 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy60 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy60 line36
to share logic between them. copy60 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy60 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy60 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy60 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy60 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy60 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy60 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy60 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy60 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy60 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy60 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy60 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy60 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy60 line51
unambiguous way. copy60 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy60 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy60 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy60 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy60 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy60 line58

** Architecture copy60 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy60 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy60 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy60 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy60 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy60 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy60 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy60 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy60 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy60 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy60 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy60 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy60 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy60 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy60 line78
=org=. copy60 line79

* Project State copy60 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy60 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy60 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy60 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy60 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy60 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy60 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy60 line91
      - [X] Links copy60 line92
      - [X] Text links copy60 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy60 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy60 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy60 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy60 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy60 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy60 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy60 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy60 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy60 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy60 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy60 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy60 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy60 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy60 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy60 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy60 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy60 line112

* Development copy60 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy60 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy60 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy60 line118
suite expectations. copy60 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy60 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy60 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy60 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy60 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy60 line125

** Prerequisites copy60 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy60 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy60 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy60 line132

** Testing copy60 line134

Running the tests: copy60 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy60 line138
# Clojure copy60 line139
lein test copy60 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy60 line141
npm install copy60 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy60 line143
lein doo node once copy60 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy60 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy60 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy60 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy60 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy60 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy60 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy60 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy60 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy60 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy60 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy60 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy60 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy60 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy60 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy60 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy60 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy60 line174
lein deps copy60 line175
npm install copy60 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy60 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy60 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy60 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy60 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy60 line183
lein test copy60 line184
lein doo node once copy60 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy60 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy60 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy60 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy60 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy60 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy60 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy60 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy60 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy60 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy60 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy60 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy60 line201
a Lisp before. copy60 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy60 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy60 line206

** What does this project do? copy62 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy62 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy62 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy62 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy62 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy62 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy62 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy62 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy62 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy62 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy62 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy62 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy62 line26
   within Emacs. copy62 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy62 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy62 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy62 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy62 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy62 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy62 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy62 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy62 line36
to share logic between them. copy62 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy62 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy62 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy62 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy62 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy62 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy62 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy62 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy62 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy62 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy62 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy62 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy62 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy62 line51
unambiguous way. copy62 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy62 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy62 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy62 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy62 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy62 line58

** Architecture copy62 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy62 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy62 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy62 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy62 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy62 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy62 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy62 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy62 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy62 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy62 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy62 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy62 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy62 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy62 line78
=org=. copy62 line79

* Project State copy62 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy62 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy62 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy62 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy62 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy62 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy62 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy62 line91
      - [X] Links copy62 line92
      - [X] Text links copy62 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy62 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy62 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy62 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy62 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy62 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy62 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy62 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy62 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy62 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy62 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy62 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy62 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy62 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy62 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy62 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy62 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy62 line112

* Development copy62 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy62 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy62 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy62 line118
suite expectations. copy62 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy62 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy62 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy62 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy62 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy62 line125

** Prerequisites copy62 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy62 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy62 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy62 line132

** Testing copy62 line134

Running the tests: copy62 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy62 line138
# Clojure copy62 line139
lein test copy62 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy62 line141
npm install copy62 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy62 line143
lein doo node once copy62 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy62 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy62 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy62 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy62 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy62 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy62 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy62 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy62 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy62 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy62 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy62 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy62 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy62 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy62 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy62 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy62 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy62 line174
lein deps copy62 line175
npm install copy62 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy62 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy62 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy62 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy62 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy62 line183
lein test copy62 line184
lein doo node once copy62 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy62 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy62 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy62 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy62 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy62 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy62 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy62 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy62 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy62 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy62 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy62 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy62 line201
a Lisp before. copy62 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy62 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy62 line206

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy64 line6

** What does this project do? copy64 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy64 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy64 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy64 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy64 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy64 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy64 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy64 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy64 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy64 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy64 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy64 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy64 line26
   within Emacs. copy64 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy64 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy64 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy64 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy64 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy64 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy64 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy64 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy64 line36
to share logic between them. copy64 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy64 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy64 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy64 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy64 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy64 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy64 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy64 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy64 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy64 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy64 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy64 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy64 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy64 line51
unambiguous way. copy64 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy64 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy64 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy64 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy64 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy64 line58

** Architecture copy64 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy64 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy64 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy64 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy64 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy64 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy64 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy64 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy64 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy64 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy64 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy64 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy64 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy64 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy64 line78
=org=. copy64 line79

* Project State copy64 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy64 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy64 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy64 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy64 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy64 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy64 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy64 line91
      - [X] Links copy64 line92
      - [X] Text links copy64 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy64 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy64 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy64 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy64 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy64 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy64 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy64 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy64 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy64 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy64 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy64 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy64 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy64 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy64 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy64 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy64 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy64 line112

* Development copy64 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy64 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy64 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy64 line118
suite expectations. copy64 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy64 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy64 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy64 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy64 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy64 line125

** Prerequisites copy64 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy64 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy64 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy64 line132

lein doo node once copy64 line144

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy64 line180

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy64 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy64 line193

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy64 line195

capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy65 line110

* Development copy65 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy65 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy65 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy65 line118
suite expectations. copy65 line119

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy65 line195

** What does this project do? copy66 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy66 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy66 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy66 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy66 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy66 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy66 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy66 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy66 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy66 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy66 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy66 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy66 line26
   within Emacs. copy66 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy66 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy66 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy66 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy66 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy66 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy66 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy66 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy66 line36
to share logic between them. copy66 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy66 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy66 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy66 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy66 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy66 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy66 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy66 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy66 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy66 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy66 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy66 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy66 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy66 line51
unambiguous way. copy66 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy66 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy66 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy66 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy66 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy66 line58

** Architecture copy66 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy66 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy66 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy66 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy66 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy66 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy66 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy66 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy66 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy66 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy66 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy66 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy66 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy66 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy66 line78
=org=. copy66 line79

* Project State copy66 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy66 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy66 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy66 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy66 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy66 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy66 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy66 line91
      - [X] Links copy66 line92
      - [X] Text links copy66 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy66 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy66 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy66 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy66 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy66 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy66 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy66 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy66 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy66 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy66 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy66 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy66 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy66 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy66 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy66 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy66 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy66 line112

* Development copy66 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy66 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy66 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy66 line118
suite expectations. copy66 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy66 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy66 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy66 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy66 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy66 line125

* Project State copy67 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy67 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy67 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy67 line86

      - [X] Headlines copy67 line89

      features that are already supported by the parser. copy67 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy67 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy67 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy67 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy67 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy67 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy67 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy67 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy67 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy67 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy67 line112

* Development copy67 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy67 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy67 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy67 line118
suite expectations. copy67 line119

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy67 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy67 line173

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy67 line192

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy67 line195

** What does this project do? copy68 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy68 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy68 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy68 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy68 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy68 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy68 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy68 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy68 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy68 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy68 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy68 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy68 line26
   within Emacs. copy68 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy68 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy68 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy68 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy68 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy68 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy68 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy68 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy68 line36
to share logic between them. copy68 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy68 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy68 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy68 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy68 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy68 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy68 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy68 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy68 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy68 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy68 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy68 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy68 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy68 line51
unambiguous way. copy68 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy68 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy68 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy68 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy68 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy68 line58

** Architecture copy68 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy68 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy68 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy68 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy68 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy68 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy68 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy68 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy68 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy68 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy68 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy68 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy68 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy68 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy68 line78
=org=. copy68 line79

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy68 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy68 line84

E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy68 line104

EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy68 line106

syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy69 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy69 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy69 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy69 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy69 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy69 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy69 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy69 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy69 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy69 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy69 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy69 line51
unambiguous way. copy69 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy69 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy69 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy69 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy69 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy69 line58

** Architecture copy69 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy69 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy69 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy69 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy69 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy69 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy69 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy69 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy69 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy69 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy69 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy69 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy69 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy69 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy69 line78
=org=. copy69 line79

* Project State copy69 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy69 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy69 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy69 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy69 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy69 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy69 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy69 line91
      - [X] Links copy69 line92
      - [X] Text links copy69 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy69 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy69 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy69 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy69 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy69 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy69 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy69 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy69 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy69 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy69 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy69 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy69 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy69 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy69 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy69 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy69 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy69 line112

* Development copy69 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy69 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy69 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy69 line118
suite expectations. copy69 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy69 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy69 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy69 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy69 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy69 line125

** Prerequisites copy69 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy69 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy69 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy69 line132

** Testing copy69 line134

Running the tests: copy69 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy69 line138

lein test copy69 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy69 line141

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy69 line143
lein doo node once copy69 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy69 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy69 line149

;; discover supported parser start rules copy69 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy69 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy69 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy69 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy69 line158

#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy69 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy69 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy69 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy69 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy69 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy69 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy69 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy69 line174
lein deps copy69 line175
npm install copy69 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy69 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy69 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy69 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy69 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy69 line183
lein test copy69 line184
lein doo node once copy69 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy69 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy69 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy69 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy69 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy69 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy69 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy69 line195

developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy69 line199

** What does this project do? copy70 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy70 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy70 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy70 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy70 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy70 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy70 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy70 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy70 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy70 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy70 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy70 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy70 line26
   within Emacs. copy70 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy70 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy70 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy70 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy70 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy70 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy70 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy70 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy70 line36
to share logic between them. copy70 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy70 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy70 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy70 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy70 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy70 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy70 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy70 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy70 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy70 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy70 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy70 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy70 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy70 line51
unambiguous way. copy70 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy70 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy70 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy70 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy70 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy70 line58

** Architecture copy70 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy70 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy70 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy70 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy70 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy70 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy70 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy70 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy70 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy70 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy70 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy70 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy70 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy70 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy70 line78
=org=. copy70 line79

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy70 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy70 line84

E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy70 line104

EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy70 line106

** What does this project do? copy71 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy71 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy71 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy71 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy71 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy71 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy71 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy71 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy71 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy71 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy71 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy71 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy71 line26
   within Emacs. copy71 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy71 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy71 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy71 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy71 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy71 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy71 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy71 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy71 line36
to share logic between them. copy71 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy71 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy71 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy71 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy71 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy71 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy71 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy71 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy71 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy71 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy71 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy71 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy71 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy71 line51
unambiguous way. copy71 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy71 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy71 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy71 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy71 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy71 line58

** Architecture copy71 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy71 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy71 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy71 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy71 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy71 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy71 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy71 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy71 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy71 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy71 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy71 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy71 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy71 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy71 line78
=org=. copy71 line79

* Project State copy71 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy71 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy71 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy71 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy71 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy71 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy71 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy71 line91
      - [X] Links copy71 line92
      - [X] Text links copy71 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy71 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy71 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy71 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy71 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy71 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy71 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy71 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy71 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy71 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy71 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy71 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy71 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy71 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy71 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy71 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy71 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy71 line112

* Development copy71 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy71 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy71 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy71 line118
suite expectations. copy71 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy71 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy71 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy71 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy71 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy71 line125

** Prerequisites copy71 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy71 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy71 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy71 line132

** Testing copy71 line134

Running the tests: copy71 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy71 line138
# Clojure copy71 line139
lein test copy71 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy71 line141
npm install copy71 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy71 line143
lein doo node once copy71 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy71 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy71 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy71 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy71 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy71 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy71 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy71 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy71 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy71 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy71 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy71 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy71 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy71 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy71 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy71 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy71 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy71 line174
lein deps copy71 line175
npm install copy71 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy71 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy71 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy71 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy71 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy71 line183
lein test copy71 line184
lein doo node once copy71 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy71 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy71 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy71 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy71 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy71 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy71 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy71 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy71 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy71 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy71 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy71 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy71 line201
a Lisp before. copy71 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy71 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy71 line206

* Usage copy71 line225
  :PROPERTIES:
  :CUSTOM_ID: usage copy71 line227
  :END:

At the moment, you can run =org-parser= from Clojure, ClojureScript, copy71 line230
or Java. Other targets which are hosted on the JVM or on JavaScript copy71 line231
are possible. copy71 line232

** Clojure Library copy71 line234

 #+BEGIN_SRC clojure :exports both copy71 line236
   (ns hello-world.core copy71 line237
     (:require [org-parser.parser :refer [parse]] copy71 line238
               [org-parser.core :refer [read-str write-str]])) copy71 line239

   (prn (parse "* Headline")) copy71 line241
   (prn (read-str "* Headline")) copy71 line242
   (println (write-str (read-str "* Headline"))) copy71 line243
 #+END_SRC

 | "* Headline\n"                                                                                |

** What does this project do? copy73 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy73 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy73 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy73 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy73 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy73 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy73 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy73 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy73 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy73 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy73 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy73 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy73 line26
   within Emacs. copy73 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy73 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy73 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy73 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy73 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy73 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy73 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy73 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy73 line36
to share logic between them. copy73 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy73 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy73 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy73 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy73 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy73 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy73 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy73 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy73 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy73 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy73 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy73 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy73 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy73 line51
unambiguous way. copy73 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy73 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy73 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy73 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy73 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy73 line58

** Architecture copy73 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy73 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy73 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy73 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy73 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy73 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy73 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy73 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy73 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy73 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy73 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy73 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy73 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy73 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy73 line78
=org=. copy73 line79

* Project State copy73 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy73 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy73 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy73 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy73 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy73 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy73 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy73 line91
      - [X] Links copy73 line92
      - [X] Text links copy73 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy73 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy73 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy73 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy73 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy73 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy73 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy73 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy73 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy73 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy73 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy73 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy73 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy73 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy73 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy73 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy73 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy73 line112

* Development copy73 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy73 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy73 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy73 line118
suite expectations. copy73 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy73 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy73 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy73 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy73 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy73 line125

** Prerequisites copy73 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy73 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy73 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy73 line132

** Testing copy73 line134

Running the tests: copy73 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy73 line138
# Clojure copy73 line139
lein test copy73 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy73 line141
npm install copy73 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy73 line143
lein doo node once copy73 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy73 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy73 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy73 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy73 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy73 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy73 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy73 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy73 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy73 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy73 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy73 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy73 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy73 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy73 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy73 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy73 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy73 line174
lein deps copy73 line175
npm install copy73 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy73 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy73 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy73 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy73 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy73 line183
lein test copy73 line184
lein doo node once copy73 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy73 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy73 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy73 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy73 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy73 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy73 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy73 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy73 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy73 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy73 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy73 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy73 line201
a Lisp before. copy73 line202
