Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy75 line6

** What does this project do? copy75 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy75 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy75 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy75 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy75 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy75 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy75 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy75 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy75 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy75 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy75 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy75 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy75 line26
   within Emacs. copy75 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy75 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy75 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy75 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy75 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy75 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy75 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy75 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy75 line36
to share logic between them. copy75 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy75 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy75 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy75 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy75 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy75 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy75 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy75 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy75 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy75 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy75 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy75 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy75 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy75 line51
unambiguous way. copy75 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy75 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy75 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy75 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy75 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy75 line58

** Architecture copy75 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy75 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy75 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy75 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy75 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy75 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy75 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy75 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy75 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy75 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy75 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy75 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy75 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy75 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy75 line78
=org=. copy75 line79

* Project State copy75 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy75 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy75 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy75 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy75 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy75 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy75 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy75 line91
      - [X] Links copy75 line92
      - [X] Text links copy75 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy75 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy75 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy75 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy75 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy75 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy75 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy75 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy75 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy75 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy75 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy75 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy75 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy75 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy75 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy75 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy75 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy75 line112

* Development copy75 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy75 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy75 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy75 line118
suite expectations. copy75 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy75 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy75 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy75 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy75 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy75 line125

** Prerequisites copy75 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy75 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy75 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy75 line132

** Testing copy75 line134

Running the tests: copy75 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy75 line138
# Clojure copy75 line139
lein test copy75 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy75 line141
npm install copy75 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy75 line143
lein doo node once copy75 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy75 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy75 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy75 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy75 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy75 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy75 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy75 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy75 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy75 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy75 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy75 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy75 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy75 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy75 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy75 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy75 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy75 line174
lein deps copy75 line175
npm install copy75 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy75 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy75 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy75 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy75 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy75 line183
lein test copy75 line184
lein doo node once copy75 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy75 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy75 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy75 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy75 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy75 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy75 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy75 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy75 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy75 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy75 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy75 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy75 line201
a Lisp before. copy75 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy75 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy75 line206

** What does this project do? copy77 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy77 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy77 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy77 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy77 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy77 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy77 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy77 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy77 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy77 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy77 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy77 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy77 line26
   within Emacs. copy77 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy77 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy77 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy77 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy77 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy77 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy77 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy77 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy77 line36
to share logic between them. copy77 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy77 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy77 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy77 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy77 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy77 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy77 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy77 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy77 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy77 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy77 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy77 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy77 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy77 line51
unambiguous way. copy77 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy77 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy77 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy77 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy77 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy77 line58

** Architecture copy77 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy77 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy77 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy77 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy77 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy77 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy77 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy77 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy77 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy77 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy77 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy77 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy77 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy77 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy77 line78
=org=. copy77 line79

* Project State copy77 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy77 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy77 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy77 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy77 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy77 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy77 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy77 line91
      - [X] Links copy77 line92
      - [X] Text links copy77 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy77 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy77 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy77 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy77 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy77 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy77 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy77 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy77 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy77 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy77 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy77 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy77 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy77 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy77 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy77 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy77 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy77 line112

* Development copy77 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy77 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy77 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy77 line118
suite expectations. copy77 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy77 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy77 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy77 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy77 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy77 line125

** Prerequisites copy77 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy77 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy77 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy77 line132

** Testing copy77 line134

Running the tests: copy77 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy77 line138
# Clojure copy77 line139
lein test copy77 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy77 line141
npm install copy77 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy77 line143
lein doo node once copy77 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy77 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy77 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy77 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy77 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy77 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy77 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy77 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy77 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy77 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy77 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy77 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy77 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy77 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy77 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy77 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy77 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy77 line174
lein deps copy77 line175
npm install copy77 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy77 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy77 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy77 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy77 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy77 line183
lein test copy77 line184
lein doo node once copy77 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy77 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy77 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy77 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy77 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy77 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy77 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy77 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy79 line6

** What does this project do? copy79 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy79 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy79 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy79 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy79 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy79 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy79 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy79 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy79 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy79 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy79 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy79 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy79 line26
   within Emacs. copy79 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy79 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy79 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy79 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy79 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy79 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy79 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy79 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy79 line36
to share logic between them. copy79 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy79 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy79 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy79 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy79 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy79 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy79 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy79 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy79 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy79 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy79 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy79 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy79 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy79 line51
unambiguous way. copy79 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy79 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy79 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy79 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy79 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy79 line58

** Architecture copy79 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy79 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy79 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy79 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy79 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy79 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy79 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy79 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy79 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy79 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy79 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy79 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy79 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy79 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy79 line78
=org=. copy79 line79

* Project State copy79 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy79 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy79 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy79 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy79 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy79 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy79 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy79 line91
      - [X] Links copy79 line92
      - [X] Text links copy79 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy79 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy79 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy79 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy79 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy79 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy79 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy79 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy79 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy79 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy79 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy79 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy79 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy79 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy79 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy79 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy79 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy79 line112

* Development copy79 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy79 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy79 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy79 line118
suite expectations. copy79 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy79 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy79 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy79 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy79 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy79 line125

** Prerequisites copy79 line127

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy79 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy79 line132

E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy80 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy80 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy80 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy80 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy80 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy80 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy80 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy80 line112

* Development copy80 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy80 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy80 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy80 line118
suite expectations. copy80 line119

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy80 line162

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy80 line192

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy80 line195

** What does this project do? copy81 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy81 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy81 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy81 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy81 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy81 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy81 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy81 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy81 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy81 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy81 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy81 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy81 line26
   within Emacs. copy81 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy81 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy81 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy81 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy81 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy81 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy81 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy81 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy81 line36
to share logic between them. copy81 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy81 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy81 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy81 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy81 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy81 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy81 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy81 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy81 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy81 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy81 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy81 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy81 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy81 line51
unambiguous way. copy81 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy81 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy81 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy81 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy81 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy81 line58

** Architecture copy81 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy81 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy81 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy81 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy81 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy81 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy81 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy81 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy81 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy81 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy81 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy81 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy81 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy81 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy81 line78
=org=. copy81 line79

* Project State copy81 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy81 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy81 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy81 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy81 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy81 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy81 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy81 line91
      - [X] Links copy81 line92
      - [X] Text links copy81 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy81 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy81 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy81 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy81 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy81 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy81 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy81 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy81 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy81 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy81 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy81 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy81 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy81 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy81 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy81 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy81 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy81 line112

* Development copy81 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy81 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy81 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy81 line118
suite expectations. copy81 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy81 line121

[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy81 line123

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy82 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy82 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy82 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy82 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy82 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy82 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy82 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy82 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy82 line78
=org=. copy82 line79

* Project State copy82 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy82 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy82 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy82 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy82 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy82 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy82 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy82 line91
      - [X] Links copy82 line92
      - [X] Text links copy82 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy82 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy82 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy82 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy82 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy82 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy82 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy82 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy82 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy82 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy82 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy82 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy82 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy82 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy82 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy82 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy82 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy82 line112

* Development copy82 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy82 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy82 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy82 line118
suite expectations. copy82 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy82 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy82 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy82 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy82 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy82 line125

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy82 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy82 line132

** Testing copy82 line134

# CLJS (single run) copy82 line141

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy82 line155

;; parse specific start rule copy82 line158

#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy82 line162

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy82 line166

** Contributor Workflow copy82 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy82 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy82 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy82 line174
lein deps copy82 line175
npm install copy82 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy82 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy82 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy82 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy82 line182

lein doo node once copy82 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy82 line188

  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy82 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy82 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy82 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy82 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy82 line195

** What does this project do? copy83 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy83 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy83 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy83 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy83 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy83 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy83 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy83 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy83 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy83 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy83 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy83 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy83 line26
   within Emacs. copy83 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy83 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy83 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy83 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy83 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy83 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy83 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy83 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy83 line36
to share logic between them. copy83 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy83 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy83 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy83 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy83 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy83 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy83 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy83 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy83 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy83 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy83 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy83 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy83 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy83 line51
unambiguous way. copy83 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy83 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy83 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy83 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy83 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy83 line58

** Architecture copy83 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy83 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy83 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy83 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy83 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy83 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy83 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy83 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy83 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy83 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy83 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy83 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy83 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy83 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy83 line78
=org=. copy83 line79

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy83 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy83 line84

*It can already be useful* for you: copy83 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy83 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy83 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy83 line106

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy83 line116

JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy83 line118

org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy84 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy84 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy84 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy84 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy84 line36
to share logic between them. copy84 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy84 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy84 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy84 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy84 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy84 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy84 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy84 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy84 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy84 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy84 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy84 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy84 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy84 line51
unambiguous way. copy84 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy84 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy84 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy84 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy84 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy84 line58

** Architecture copy84 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy84 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy84 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy84 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy84 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy84 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy84 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy84 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy84 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy84 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy84 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy84 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy84 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy84 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy84 line78
=org=. copy84 line79

* Project State copy84 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy84 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy84 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy84 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy84 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy84 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy84 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy84 line91
      - [X] Links copy84 line92
      - [X] Text links copy84 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy84 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy84 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy84 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy84 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy84 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy84 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy84 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy84 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy84 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy84 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy84 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy84 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy84 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy84 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy84 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy84 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy84 line112

* Development copy84 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy84 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy84 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy84 line118
suite expectations. copy84 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy84 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy84 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy84 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy84 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy84 line125

** Prerequisites copy84 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy84 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy84 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy84 line132

** Testing copy84 line134

Running the tests: copy84 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy84 line138
# Clojure copy84 line139
lein test copy84 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy84 line141
npm install copy84 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy84 line143
lein doo node once copy84 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy84 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy84 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy84 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy84 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy84 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy84 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy84 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy84 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy84 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy84 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy84 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy84 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy84 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy84 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy84 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy84 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy84 line174
lein deps copy84 line175
npm install copy84 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy84 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy84 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy84 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy84 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy84 line183
lein test copy84 line184
lein doo node once copy84 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy84 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy84 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy84 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy84 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy84 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy84 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy84 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy84 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy84 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy84 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy84 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy84 line201

** What does this project do? copy85 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy85 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy85 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy85 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy85 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy85 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy85 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy85 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy85 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy85 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy85 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy85 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy85 line26
   within Emacs. copy85 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy85 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy85 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy85 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy85 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy85 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy85 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy85 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy85 line36
to share logic between them. copy85 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy85 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy85 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy85 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy85 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy85 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy85 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy85 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy85 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy85 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy85 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy85 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy85 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy85 line51
unambiguous way. copy85 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy85 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy85 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy85 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy85 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy85 line58

** Architecture copy85 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy85 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy85 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy85 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy85 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy85 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy85 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy85 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy85 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy85 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy85 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy85 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy85 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy85 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy85 line78
=org=. copy85 line79

* Project State copy85 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy85 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy85 line84

*It can already be useful* for you: copy85 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy85 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy85 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy85 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy85 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy85 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy85 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy85 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy85 line112

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy85 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy85 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy85 line118
suite expectations. copy85 line119

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy86 line6

** What does this project do? copy86 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy86 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy86 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy86 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy86 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy86 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy86 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy86 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy86 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy86 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy86 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy86 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy86 line26
   within Emacs. copy86 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy86 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy86 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy86 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy86 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy86 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy86 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy86 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy86 line36
to share logic between them. copy86 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy86 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy86 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy86 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy86 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy86 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy86 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy86 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy86 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy86 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy86 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy86 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy86 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy86 line51
unambiguous way. copy86 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy86 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy86 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy86 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy86 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy86 line58

** Architecture copy86 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy86 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy86 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy86 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy86 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy86 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy86 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy86 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy86 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy86 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy86 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy86 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy86 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy86 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy86 line78
=org=. copy86 line79

* Project State copy86 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy86 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy86 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy86 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy86 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy86 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy86 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy86 line91
      - [X] Links copy86 line92
      - [X] Text links copy86 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy86 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy86 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy86 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy86 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy86 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy86 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy86 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy86 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy86 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy86 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy86 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy86 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy86 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy86 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy86 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy86 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy86 line112

* Development copy86 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy86 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy86 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy86 line118
suite expectations. copy86 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy86 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy86 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy86 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy86 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy86 line125

** Prerequisites copy86 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy86 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy86 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy86 line132

** Testing copy86 line134

Running the tests: copy86 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy86 line138
# Clojure copy86 line139
lein test copy86 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy86 line141
npm install copy86 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy86 line143
lein doo node once copy86 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy86 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy86 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy86 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy86 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy86 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy86 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy86 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy86 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy86 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy86 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy86 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy86 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy86 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy86 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy86 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy86 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy86 line174
lein deps copy86 line175
npm install copy86 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy86 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy86 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy86 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy86 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy86 line183
lein test copy86 line184
lein doo node once copy86 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy86 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy86 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy86 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy86 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy86 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy86 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy86 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy86 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy86 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy86 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy86 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy86 line201
a Lisp before. copy86 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy86 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy86 line206

* Release and Dependency Information copy86 line208

* Usage copy86 line225
  :PROPERTIES:
  :CUSTOM_ID: usage copy86 line227
  :END:

At the moment, you can run =org-parser= from Clojure, ClojureScript, copy86 line230
or Java. Other targets which are hosted on the JVM or on JavaScript copy86 line231
are possible. copy86 line232

** Clojure Library copy86 line234

 #+BEGIN_SRC clojure :exports both copy86 line236
   (ns hello-world.core copy86 line237
     (:require [org-parser.parser :refer [parse]] copy86 line238
               [org-parser.core :refer [read-str write-str]])) copy86 line239

   (prn (parse "* Headline")) copy86 line241
   (prn (read-str "* Headline")) copy86 line242
   (println (write-str (read-str "* Headline"))) copy86 line243
 #+END_SRC

 | "* Headline\n"                                                                                |

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy88 line6

** What does this project do? copy88 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy88 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy88 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy88 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy88 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy88 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy88 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy88 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy88 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy88 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy88 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy88 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy88 line26
   within Emacs. copy88 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy88 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy88 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy88 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy88 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy88 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy88 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy88 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy88 line36
to share logic between them. copy88 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy88 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy88 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy88 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy88 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy88 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy88 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy88 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy88 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy88 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy88 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy88 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy88 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy88 line51
unambiguous way. copy88 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy88 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy88 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy88 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy88 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy88 line58

** Architecture copy88 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy88 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy88 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy88 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy88 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy88 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy88 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy88 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy88 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy88 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy88 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy88 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy88 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy88 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy88 line78
=org=. copy88 line79

* Project State copy88 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy88 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy88 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy88 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy88 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy88 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy88 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy88 line91
      - [X] Links copy88 line92
      - [X] Text links copy88 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy88 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy88 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy88 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy88 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy88 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy88 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy88 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy88 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy88 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy88 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy88 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy88 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy88 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy88 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy88 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy88 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy88 line112

* Development copy88 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy88 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy88 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy88 line118
suite expectations. copy88 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy88 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy88 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy88 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy88 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy88 line125

** Prerequisites copy88 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy88 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy88 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy88 line132

** Testing copy88 line134

Running the tests: copy88 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy88 line138
# Clojure copy88 line139
lein test copy88 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy88 line141
npm install copy88 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy88 line143
lein doo node once copy88 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy88 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy88 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy88 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy88 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy88 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy88 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy88 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy88 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy88 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy88 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy88 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy88 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy88 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy88 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy88 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy88 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy88 line174
lein deps copy88 line175
npm install copy88 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy88 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy88 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy88 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy88 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy88 line183
lein test copy88 line184
lein doo node once copy88 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy88 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy88 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy88 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy88 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy88 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy88 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy88 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy88 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy88 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy88 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy88 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy88 line201
a Lisp before. copy88 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy88 line204
