Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy90 line6

** What does this project do? copy90 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy90 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy90 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy90 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy90 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy90 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy90 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy90 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy90 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy90 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy90 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy90 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy90 line26
   within Emacs. copy90 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy90 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy90 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy90 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy90 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy90 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy90 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy90 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy90 line36
to share logic between them. copy90 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy90 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy90 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy90 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy90 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy90 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy90 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy90 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy90 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy90 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy90 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy90 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy90 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy90 line51
unambiguous way. copy90 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy90 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy90 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy90 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy90 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy90 line58

** Architecture copy90 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy90 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy90 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy90 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy90 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy90 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy90 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy90 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy90 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy90 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy90 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy90 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy90 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy90 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy90 line78
=org=. copy90 line79

* Project State copy90 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy90 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy90 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy90 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy90 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy90 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy90 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy90 line91
      - [X] Links copy90 line92
      - [X] Text links copy90 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy90 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy90 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy90 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy90 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy90 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy90 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy90 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy90 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy90 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy90 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy90 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy90 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy90 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy90 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy90 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy90 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy90 line112

* Development copy90 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy90 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy90 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy90 line118
suite expectations. copy90 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy90 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy90 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy90 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy90 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy90 line125

** Prerequisites copy90 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy90 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy90 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy90 line132

** Testing copy90 line134

Running the tests: copy90 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy90 line138
# Clojure copy90 line139
lein test copy90 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy90 line141
npm install copy90 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy90 line143
lein doo node once copy90 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy90 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy90 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy90 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy90 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy90 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy90 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy90 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy90 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy90 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy90 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy90 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy90 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy90 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy90 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy90 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy90 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy90 line174
lein deps copy90 line175
npm install copy90 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy90 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy90 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy90 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy90 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy90 line183
lein test copy90 line184
lein doo node once copy90 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy90 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy90 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy90 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy90 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy90 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy90 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy90 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy90 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy90 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy90 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy90 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy90 line201
a Lisp before. copy90 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy90 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy90 line206

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy91 line180

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy91 line192

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy91 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy92 line6

** What does this project do? copy92 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy92 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy92 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy92 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy92 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy92 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy92 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy92 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy92 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy92 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy92 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy92 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy92 line26
   within Emacs. copy92 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy92 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy92 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy92 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy92 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy92 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy92 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy92 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy92 line36
to share logic between them. copy92 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy92 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy92 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy92 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy92 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy92 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy92 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy92 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy92 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy92 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy92 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy92 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy92 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy92 line51
unambiguous way. copy92 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy92 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy92 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy92 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy92 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy92 line58

** Architecture copy92 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy92 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy92 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy92 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy92 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy92 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy92 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy92 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy92 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy92 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy92 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy92 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy92 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy92 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy92 line78
=org=. copy92 line79

* Project State copy92 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy92 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy92 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy92 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy92 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy92 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy92 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy92 line91
      - [X] Links copy92 line92
      - [X] Text links copy92 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy92 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy92 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy92 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy92 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy92 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy92 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy92 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy92 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy92 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy92 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy92 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy92 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy92 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy92 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy92 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy92 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy92 line112

* Development copy92 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy92 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy92 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy92 line118
suite expectations. copy92 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy92 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy92 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy92 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy92 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy92 line125

** Prerequisites copy92 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy92 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy92 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy92 line132

** Testing copy92 line134

Running the tests: copy92 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy92 line138

# CLJS (single run) copy92 line141
npm install copy92 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy92 line143
lein doo node once copy92 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy92 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy92 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy92 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy92 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy92 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy92 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy92 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy92 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy92 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy92 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy92 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy92 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy92 line166
#+END_SRC

lein deps copy92 line175
npm install copy92 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy92 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy92 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy92 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy92 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy92 line183
lein test copy92 line184
lein doo node once copy92 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy92 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy92 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy92 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy92 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy92 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy92 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy92 line195

** Prerequisites copy93 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy93 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy93 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy93 line132

** Testing copy93 line134

Running the tests: copy93 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy93 line138
# Clojure copy93 line139
lein test copy93 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy93 line141
npm install copy93 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy93 line143
lein doo node once copy93 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy93 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy93 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy93 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy93 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy93 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy93 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy93 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy93 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy93 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy93 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy93 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy93 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy93 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy93 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy93 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy93 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy93 line174
lein deps copy93 line175
npm install copy93 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy93 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy93 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy93 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy93 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy93 line183
lein test copy93 line184
lein doo node once copy93 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy93 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy93 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy93 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy93 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy93 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy93 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy93 line195

** What does this project do? copy94 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy94 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy94 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy94 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy94 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy94 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy94 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy94 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy94 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy94 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy94 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy94 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy94 line26
   within Emacs. copy94 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy94 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy94 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy94 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy94 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy94 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy94 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy94 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy94 line36
to share logic between them. copy94 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy94 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy94 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy94 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy94 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy94 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy94 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy94 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy94 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy94 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy94 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy94 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy94 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy94 line51
unambiguous way. copy94 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy94 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy94 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy94 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy94 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy94 line58

** Architecture copy94 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy94 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy94 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy94 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy94 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy94 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy94 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy94 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy94 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy94 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy94 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy94 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy94 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy94 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy94 line78
=org=. copy94 line79

* Project State copy94 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy94 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy94 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy94 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy94 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy94 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy94 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy94 line91
      - [X] Links copy94 line92
      - [X] Text links copy94 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy94 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy94 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy94 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy94 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy94 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy94 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy94 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy94 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy94 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy94 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy94 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy94 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy94 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy94 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy94 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy94 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy94 line112

* Development copy94 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy94 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy94 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy94 line118
suite expectations. copy94 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy94 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy94 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy94 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy94 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy94 line125

      features that are already supported by the parser. copy95 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy95 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy95 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy95 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy95 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy95 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy95 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy95 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy95 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy95 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy95 line112

* Development copy95 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy95 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy95 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy95 line118
suite expectations. copy95 line119

and transform boundaries are documented in copy95 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy95 line123

** Testing copy95 line134

#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy95 line162

** Contributor Workflow copy95 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy95 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy95 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy95 line174
lein deps copy95 line175
npm install copy95 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy95 line178

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy95 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy95 line182

lein doo node once copy95 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy95 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy95 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy95 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy95 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy95 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy95 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy95 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy96 line6

** What does this project do? copy96 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy96 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy96 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy96 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy96 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy96 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy96 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy96 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy96 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy96 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy96 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy96 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy96 line26
   within Emacs. copy96 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy96 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy96 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy96 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy96 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy96 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy96 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy96 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy96 line36
to share logic between them. copy96 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy96 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy96 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy96 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy96 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy96 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy96 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy96 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy96 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy96 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy96 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy96 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy96 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy96 line51
unambiguous way. copy96 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy96 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy96 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy96 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy96 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy96 line58

** Architecture copy96 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy96 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy96 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy96 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy96 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy96 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy96 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy96 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy96 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy96 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy96 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy96 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy96 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy96 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy96 line78
=org=. copy96 line79

* Project State copy96 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy96 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy96 line84

suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy97 line51
unambiguous way. copy97 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy97 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy97 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy97 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy97 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy97 line58

** Architecture copy97 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy97 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy97 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy97 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy97 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy97 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy97 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy97 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy97 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy97 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy97 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy97 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy97 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy97 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy97 line78
=org=. copy97 line79

* Project State copy97 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy97 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy97 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy97 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy97 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy97 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy97 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy97 line91
      - [X] Links copy97 line92
      - [X] Text links copy97 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy97 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy97 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy97 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy97 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy97 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy97 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy97 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy97 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy97 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy97 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy97 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy97 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy97 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy97 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy97 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy97 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy97 line112

* Development copy97 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy97 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy97 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy97 line118
suite expectations. copy97 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy97 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy97 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy97 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy97 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy97 line125

** Prerequisites copy97 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy97 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy97 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy97 line132

** Testing copy97 line134

Running the tests: copy97 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy97 line138

lein test copy97 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy97 line141

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy97 line143
lein doo node once copy97 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy97 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy97 line149

;; discover supported parser start rules copy97 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy97 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy97 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy97 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy97 line158

#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy97 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy97 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy97 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy97 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy97 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy97 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy97 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy97 line174
lein deps copy97 line175
npm install copy97 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy97 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy97 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy97 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy97 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy97 line183
lein test copy97 line184
lein doo node once copy97 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy97 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy97 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy97 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy97 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy97 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy97 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy97 line195

** What does this project do? copy98 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy98 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy98 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy98 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy98 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy98 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy98 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy98 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy98 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy98 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy98 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy98 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy98 line26
   within Emacs. copy98 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy98 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy98 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy98 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy98 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy98 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy98 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy98 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy98 line36
to share logic between them. copy98 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy98 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy98 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy98 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy98 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy98 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy98 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy98 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy98 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy98 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy98 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy98 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy98 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy98 line51
unambiguous way. copy98 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy98 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy98 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy98 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy98 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy98 line58

** Architecture copy98 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy98 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy98 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy98 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy98 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy98 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy98 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy98 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy98 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy98 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy98 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy98 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy98 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy98 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy98 line78
=org=. copy98 line79

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy98 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy98 line84

E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy98 line104

EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy98 line106

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy99 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy99 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy99 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy99 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy99 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy99 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy99 line26
   within Emacs. copy99 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy99 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy99 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy99 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy99 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy99 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy99 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy99 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy99 line36
to share logic between them. copy99 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy99 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy99 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy99 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy99 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy99 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy99 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy99 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy99 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy99 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy99 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy99 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy99 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy99 line51
unambiguous way. copy99 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy99 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy99 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy99 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy99 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy99 line58

** Architecture copy99 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy99 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy99 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy99 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy99 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy99 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy99 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy99 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy99 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy99 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy99 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy99 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy99 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy99 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy99 line78
=org=. copy99 line79

* Project State copy99 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy99 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy99 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy99 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy99 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy99 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy99 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy99 line91
      - [X] Links copy99 line92
      - [X] Text links copy99 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy99 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy99 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy99 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy99 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy99 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy99 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy99 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy99 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy99 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy99 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy99 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy99 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy99 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy99 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy99 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy99 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy99 line112

* Development copy99 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy99 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy99 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy99 line118
suite expectations. copy99 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy99 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy99 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy99 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy99 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy99 line125

** Prerequisites copy99 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy99 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy99 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy99 line132

** Testing copy99 line134

Running the tests: copy99 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy99 line138
# Clojure copy99 line139
lein test copy99 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy99 line141
npm install copy99 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy99 line143
lein doo node once copy99 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy99 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy99 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy99 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy99 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy99 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy99 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy99 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy99 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy99 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy99 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy99 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy99 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy99 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy99 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy99 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy99 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy99 line174
lein deps copy99 line175
npm install copy99 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy99 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy99 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy99 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy99 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy99 line183
lein test copy99 line184
lein doo node once copy99 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy99 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy99 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy99 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy99 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy99 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy99 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy99 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy99 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy99 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy99 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy99 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy99 line201
a Lisp before. copy99 line202

** What does this project do? copy100 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy100 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy100 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy100 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy100 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy100 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy100 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy100 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy100 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy100 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy100 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy100 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy100 line26
   within Emacs. copy100 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy100 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy100 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy100 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy100 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy100 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy100 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy100 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy100 line36
to share logic between them. copy100 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy100 line39

while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy100 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy100 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy100 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy100 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy100 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy100 line46

mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy100 line48

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy100 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy100 line55

facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy100 line57

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy101 line6

** What does this project do? copy101 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy101 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy101 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy101 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy101 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy101 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy101 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy101 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy101 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy101 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy101 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy101 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy101 line26
   within Emacs. copy101 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy101 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy101 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy101 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy101 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy101 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy101 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy101 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy101 line36
to share logic between them. copy101 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy101 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy101 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy101 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy101 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy101 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy101 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy101 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy101 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy101 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy101 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy101 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy101 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy101 line51
unambiguous way. copy101 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy101 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy101 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy101 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy101 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy101 line58

** Architecture copy101 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy101 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy101 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy101 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy101 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy101 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy101 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy101 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy101 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy101 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy101 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy101 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy101 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy101 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy101 line78
=org=. copy101 line79

* Project State copy101 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy101 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy101 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy101 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy101 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy101 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy101 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy101 line91
      - [X] Links copy101 line92
      - [X] Text links copy101 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy101 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy101 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy101 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy101 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy101 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy101 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy101 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy101 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy101 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy101 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy101 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy101 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy101 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy101 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy101 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy101 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy101 line112

* Development copy101 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy101 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy101 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy101 line118
suite expectations. copy101 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy101 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy101 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy101 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy101 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy101 line125

** Prerequisites copy101 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy101 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy101 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy101 line132

** Testing copy101 line134

Running the tests: copy101 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy101 line138
# Clojure copy101 line139
lein test copy101 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy101 line141
npm install copy101 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy101 line143
lein doo node once copy101 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy101 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy101 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy101 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy101 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy101 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy101 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy101 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy101 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy101 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy101 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy101 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy101 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy101 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy101 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy101 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy101 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy101 line174
lein deps copy101 line175
npm install copy101 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy101 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy101 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy101 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy101 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy101 line183
lein test copy101 line184
lein doo node once copy101 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy101 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy101 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy101 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy101 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy101 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy101 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy101 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy101 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy101 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy101 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy101 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy101 line201
a Lisp before. copy101 line202

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy103 line6

** What does this project do? copy103 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy103 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy103 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy103 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy103 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy103 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy103 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy103 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy103 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy103 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy103 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy103 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy103 line26
   within Emacs. copy103 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy103 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy103 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy103 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy103 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy103 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy103 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy103 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy103 line36
to share logic between them. copy103 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy103 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy103 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy103 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy103 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy103 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy103 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy103 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy103 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy103 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy103 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy103 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy103 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy103 line51
unambiguous way. copy103 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy103 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy103 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy103 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy103 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy103 line58

** Architecture copy103 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy103 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy103 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy103 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy103 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy103 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy103 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy103 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy103 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy103 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy103 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy103 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy103 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy103 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy103 line78
=org=. copy103 line79

* Project State copy103 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy103 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy103 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy103 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy103 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy103 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy103 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy103 line91
      - [X] Links copy103 line92
      - [X] Text links copy103 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy103 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy103 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy103 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy103 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy103 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy103 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy103 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy103 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy103 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy103 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy103 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy103 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy103 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy103 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy103 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy103 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy103 line112

* Development copy103 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy103 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy103 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy103 line118
suite expectations. copy103 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy103 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy103 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy103 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy103 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy103 line125

** Prerequisites copy103 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy103 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy103 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy103 line132

** Testing copy103 line134

Running the tests: copy103 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy103 line138
# Clojure copy103 line139
lein test copy103 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy103 line141
npm install copy103 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy103 line143
lein doo node once copy103 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy103 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy103 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy103 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy103 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy103 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy103 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy103 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy103 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy103 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy103 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy103 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy103 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy103 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy103 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy103 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy103 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy103 line174
lein deps copy103 line175
npm install copy103 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy103 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy103 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy103 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy103 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy103 line183
lein test copy103 line184
lein doo node once copy103 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy103 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy103 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy103 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy103 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy103 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy103 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy103 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy103 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy103 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy103 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy103 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy103 line201
a Lisp before. copy103 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy103 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy103 line206
