Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy105 line6

** What does this project do? copy105 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy105 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy105 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy105 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy105 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy105 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy105 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy105 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy105 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy105 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy105 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy105 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy105 line26
   within Emacs. copy105 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy105 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy105 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy105 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy105 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy105 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy105 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy105 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy105 line36
to share logic between them. copy105 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy105 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy105 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy105 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy105 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy105 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy105 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy105 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy105 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy105 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy105 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy105 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy105 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy105 line51
unambiguous way. copy105 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy105 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy105 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy105 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy105 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy105 line58

** Architecture copy105 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy105 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy105 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy105 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy105 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy105 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy105 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy105 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy105 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy105 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy105 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy105 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy105 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy105 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy105 line78
=org=. copy105 line79

* Project State copy105 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy105 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy105 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy105 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy105 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy105 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy105 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy105 line91
      - [X] Links copy105 line92
      - [X] Text links copy105 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy105 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy105 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy105 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy105 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy105 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy105 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy105 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy105 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy105 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy105 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy105 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy105 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy105 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy105 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy105 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy105 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy105 line112

* Development copy105 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy105 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy105 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy105 line118
suite expectations. copy105 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy105 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy105 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy105 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy105 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy105 line125

** Prerequisites copy105 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy105 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy105 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy105 line132

** Testing copy105 line134

Running the tests: copy105 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy105 line138
# Clojure copy105 line139
lein test copy105 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy105 line141
npm install copy105 line142
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy105 line143
lein doo node once copy105 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy105 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy105 line149
(require '[org-parser.parser :as parser]) copy105 line150

;; discover supported parser start rules copy105 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy105 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy105 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy105 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy105 line158
(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy105 line159
#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy105 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy105 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy105 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy105 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy105 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy105 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy105 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy105 line174
lein deps copy105 line175
npm install copy105 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy105 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy105 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy105 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy105 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy105 line183
lein test copy105 line184
lein doo node once copy105 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy105 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy105 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy105 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy105 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy105 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy105 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy105 line195

If you're not familiar with Lisp or Clojure, here's a short video on copy105 line197
how the tooling for Lisp (and hence Clojure) is great and enables fast copy105 line198
developer feedback and high quality applications. Initially, the video copy105 line199
was created to answer a [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/issues/4][specific issue]] on this repository. However, the question is a valid copy105 line200
general question that is asked quite often by people who haven't used copy105 line201
a Lisp before. copy105 line202

[[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/200ok-ch/org-parser/master/doc/images/quick_introduction_to_lisp_clojure_and_using_the_repl.jpg]] copy105 line204

You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/o2MLHFGUkoQ copy105 line206

(parser/parse "#+TODO: TODO | DONE" :start :todo-line) copy106 line159

** Contributor Workflow copy106 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy106 line171

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy106 line180

- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy106 line192

  above and check parser parity tests first. copy106 line195

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy107 line6

** What does this project do? copy107 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy107 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy107 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy107 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy107 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy107 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy107 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy107 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy107 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy107 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy107 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy107 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy107 line26
   within Emacs. copy107 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy107 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy107 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy107 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy107 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy107 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy107 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy107 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy107 line36
to share logic between them. copy107 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy107 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy107 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy107 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy107 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy107 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy107 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy107 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy107 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy107 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy107 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy107 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy107 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy107 line51
unambiguous way. copy107 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy107 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy107 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy107 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy107 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy107 line58

** Architecture copy107 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy107 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy107 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy107 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy107 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy107 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy107 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy107 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy107 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy107 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy107 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy107 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy107 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy107 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy107 line78
=org=. copy107 line79

* Project State copy107 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy107 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy107 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy107 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy107 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy107 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy107 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy107 line91
      - [X] Links copy107 line92
      - [X] Text links copy107 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy107 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy107 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy107 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy107 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy107 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy107 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy107 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy107 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy107 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy107 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy107 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy107 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy107 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy107 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy107 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy107 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy107 line112

* Development copy107 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy107 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy107 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy107 line118
suite expectations. copy107 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy107 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy107 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy107 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy107 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy107 line125

** Prerequisites copy107 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy107 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy107 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy107 line132

# CLJS (single run) copy107 line141

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy107 line143
lein doo node once copy107 line144
#+END_SRC

#+END_SRC

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy107 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy107 line182

Common troubleshooting: copy107 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy107 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy107 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy107 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy107 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy107 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy107 line195

capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy108 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy108 line112

* Development copy108 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy108 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy108 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy108 line118
suite expectations. copy108 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy108 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy108 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy108 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy108 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy108 line125

** Prerequisites copy108 line127

Please install [[https://clojure.org/guides/getting_started][Clojure]], [[https://leiningen.org/][Leiningen]], and Node.js (with npm). copy108 line129

Leiningen will pull JVM dependencies. For CLJS/node tests, install copy108 line131
JavaScript dependencies with =npm install=. copy108 line132

** Testing copy108 line134

Running the tests: copy108 line136

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy108 line138
# Clojure copy108 line139
lein test copy108 line140
# CLJS (single run) copy108 line141

./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy108 line143
lein doo node once copy108 line144
#+END_SRC

Parser API quick checks: copy108 line147

#+BEGIN_SRC clojure copy108 line149

;; discover supported parser start rules copy108 line152
(parser/supported-start-rules) copy108 line153

;; parse an entire document (default start rule :S) copy108 line155
(parser/parse "* headline\nbody") copy108 line156

;; parse specific start rule copy108 line158

#+END_SRC

Generate ANTLR parser artifacts (required for ANTLR backend work): copy108 line162

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy108 line164
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy108 line165
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy108 line166
#+END_SRC

** Contributor Workflow copy108 line169

For parser/grammar changes, use this sequence: copy108 line171

#+BEGIN_SRC shell copy108 line173
# 1) Install dependencies copy108 line174
lein deps copy108 line175
npm install copy108 line176

# 2) Regenerate parser artifacts copy108 line178
./script/gen-antlr.sh copy108 line179
./script/gen-antlr-js.sh copy108 line180

# 3) Run static checks and tests copy108 line182
clj-kondo --lint src test --parallel copy108 line183
lein test copy108 line184
lein doo node once copy108 line185
#+END_SRC

Common troubleshooting: copy108 line188

- If CLJ tests fail with missing generated classes, run copy108 line190
  =./script/gen-antlr.sh=. copy108 line191
- If CLJS tests fail with missing JS parser runtime modules, run copy108 line192
  =npm install= and =./script/gen-antlr-js.sh=. copy108 line193
- If parser behavior differs between runtimes, run both test commands copy108 line194
  above and check parser parity tests first. copy108 line195

#+html: <p>Tests: <a href="https://circleci.com/gh/200ok-ch/org-parser"><img src="https://circleci.com/gh/200ok-ch/org-parser.svg?style=svg"></a></p> copy109 line2

Community chat: #organice on IRC [[https://libera.chat/][Libera.Chat]], or [[https://matrix.to/#/!DfVpGxoYxpbfAhuimY:matrix.org?via=matrix.org&via=ungleich.ch][#organice:matrix.org]] on Matrix copy109 line6

** What does this project do? copy109 line8

=org-parser= is a parser for the [[https://orgmode.org/][Org mode]] markup language for Emacs. copy109 line10

It can be used from JavaScript, Java, Clojure and ClojureScript! copy109 line12

** Why is this project useful / Rationale copy109 line14

Org mode in Emacs is implemented in [[http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/emacs/org-mode.git/tree/lisp/org-element.el][org-element.el]] ([[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-element-api.html][API copy109 line16
documentation]]). The [[https://orgmode.org/worg/dev/org-syntax.html][spec for the Org syntax is written in prose]]. copy109 line17

This is already great work, yet it has some drawbacks: copy109 line19

1. The spec is not machine readable. Hence, there can be drift between copy109 line21
   documentation and implementation. In fact, during the development copy109 line22
   of [[https://github.com/200ok-ch/organice/][organice]], our web-based Org implementation with great mobile copy109 line23
   phone support, and =org-parser= we have encountered drift. copy109 line24
2. org-element.el is naturally written in Emacs lisp and makes strong copy109 line25
   use of Emacs as a text-processor. Hence, its code can only be used copy109 line26
   within Emacs. copy109 line27

While writing the official spec already is an amazing effort in the copy109 line29
standardization of the Org format, the power of Org is so enticing copy109 line30
that many want to use it outside of Emacs, as well. Since copy109 line31
org-element.el only runs in Emacs, this caused a myriad of copy109 line32
implementations for other platforms (JavaScript, Rust, Go, Java, etc) copy109 line33
to have been created. Most implementations are only partial, and more copy109 line34
importantly each of them creates another island. Since they are just copy109 line35
as programming language dependent as org-element.el, it is impossible copy109 line36
to share logic between them. copy109 line37

=org-parser= aims at alleviating both these issues. It specifies the copy109 line39
syntax in ANTLR grammars, which remain close to EBNF-style notation copy109 line40
while giving us a mature parser toolchain, robust error handling, and copy109 line41
multi-runtime generation for JVM and JavaScript. The reference copy109 line42
implementation is done in a way that it runs on the established copy109 line43
virtual machines of Java and JavaScript. Hence, copy109 line44
=org-parser= can be used from all programming languages running on copy109 line45
those virtual machines. =org-parser= provides a higher-level data copy109 line46
structure that is easy to consume for an application working with Org copy109 line47
mode data. Even if your application is not running on the Java or copy109 line48
JavaScript virtual machines, you can embed =org-parser= as a copy109 line49
command-line application. Lastly, =org-parser= brings a strong test copy109 line50
suite to document the reference implementation in yet another copy109 line51
unambiguous way. copy109 line52

It is our aim that =org-parser= can be the foundation on which many copy109 line54
Org mode applications in many different languages can be built. The copy109 line55
applications using =org-parser= can then focus on implementing user copy109 line56
facing features and don’t have to worry about the implementation of copy109 line57
the Org syntax itself. copy109 line58

** Architecture copy109 line60

The code base of =org-parser= is split into four namespaces: copy109 line62

- org-parser.core (top level API, i.e. =read-str=, =write-str=) copy109 line64
- org-parser.parser (parser API and parser contract wrappers) copy109 line65
- org-parser.transform (transforms parser AST into a higher-level structure) copy109 line66
- org-parser.render (serializer/writer) copy109 line67

ANTLR currently parses document and line-level structure. Several copy109 line69
inline constructs (for example parts of text, links, timestamps, and copy109 line70
table normalization) are still handled by direct parser helpers after copy109 line71
ANTLR returns a structural AST. This split is intentional for now and copy109 line72
is documented further in [[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy109 line73

Thus =org-parser= has become a misnomer in the sense, that it now copy109 line75
strives to be =clojure/data.org= (after the pattern of existing Clojure copy109 line76
libraries like =data.json=, =data.xml=, =data.csv=, etc) providing copy109 line77
reader as well as writer capabilities for the serialization format copy109 line78
=org=. copy109 line79

* Project State copy109 line81

This project is work-in-progress. It is not ready for production yet copy109 line83
because the structure of the AST (parse tree) can still change. copy109 line84

The biggest milestones are: copy109 line86

- [X] Finish ANTLR grammar/parser coverage for most Org mode syntax copy109 line88
      - [X] Headlines copy109 line89
      - [X] Org mode =#+*= stuff copy109 line90
      - [X] Timestamps copy109 line91
      - [X] Links copy109 line92
      - [X] Text links copy109 line93
      - [X] Footnotes copy109 line94
      - [X] Styled text copy109 line95
      - [X] Drawers and =#+BEGIN_xxx= blocks copy109 line96
      - [ ] Nested markup (see #12) copy109 line97
- [X] Setup basic transformation from the parse tree to a higher-level structure. copy109 line98
- [-] Transformations to higher-level structure: catch up with copy109 line99
      features that are already supported by the parser. copy109 line100
- [-] Render parsed org file with =write-str= copy109 line101

*It can already be useful* for you: copy109 line103
E.g. if your script needs to parse parts of Org mode features, our copy109 line104
ANTLR grammar/parser probably already supports that. The grammar stays copy109 line105
EBNF-like and readable, while ANTLR gives us strong tooling across CLJ copy109 line106
and CLJS runtimes. Do not underestimate copy109 line107
e.g. timestamps. Use our well-tested parser to disassemble it in its copy109 line108
parts, instead of trying to write a poor and ugly regex that is only copy109 line109
capable of a subset of Org mode's timestamps ;) copy109 line110

Don't hesitate to contribute! copy109 line112

* Development copy109 line114

=org-parser= uses ANTLR-generated parsers as the default backend on copy109 line116
both CLJ and CLJS. This keeps grammar behavior aligned across JVM and copy109 line117
JavaScript runtimes while preserving the existing public API and test copy109 line118
suite expectations. copy109 line119

For maintainers: parser layering, runtime adapters, normalization, copy109 line121
and transform boundaries are documented in copy109 line122
[[file:doc/parser-architecture.md][doc/parser-architecture.md]]. copy109 line123
Grammar scope and future migration candidates are tracked in copy109 line124
[[file:doc/grammar-roadmap.md][doc/grammar-roadmap.md]]. copy109 line125
